meta.description: Comprehensive data model for 3D visualization of world religious and mythological traditions, their evolution, relationships, and influences.

dataStructure.traditions: Array of religious/mythological traditions with properties for visualization
dataStructure.connections: Array of directional relationships between traditions
dataStructure.syncreticNodes: Array of syncretic fusion points where traditions merged
dataStructure.scholarlyFrameworks: Reference frameworks for understanding religious evolution
dataStructure.timelineEras: Major historical periods for temporal positioning

keyFigures.role: Lesser deities/spirits

traditions.name: Santeria (Regla de Ocha)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 16th-19th century (developed during slavery)

keyFigures.name: Orishas
keyFigures.role: Yoruba deities syncreted with Catholic saints

keyFigures.name: Shango/Santa Barbara
keyFigures.role: Thunder orisha syncreted with St. Barbara

keyFigures.name: Yemaya/Our Lady of Regla
keyFigures.role: Ocean mother syncreted with Virgin Mary

keyFigures.name: Ogun/St. Peter
keyFigures.role: Iron/war orisha syncreted with St. Peter

traditions.name: Candomble
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 16th-19th century (developed during slavery)

traditions.name: Haitian Vodou
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 17th-18th century (developed during slavery)

keyFigures.name: Bondye
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator (from French 'Bon Dieu')

keyFigures.name: Lwa/Loa
keyFigures.role: Spirits/intermediaries

keyFigures.name: Papa Legba
keyFigures.role: Gatekeeper spirit (from Yoruba Eshu)

keyFigures.name: Baron Samedi
keyFigures.role: Lwa of death

keyFigures.name: Erzulie Freda
keyFigures.role: Lwa of love

traditions.name: Gnosticism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1st-2nd century CE

keyFigures.name: Demiurge/Yaldabaoth
keyFigures.role: Ignorant creator of material world

keyFigures.name: Sophia
keyFigures.role: Divine wisdom, whose fall caused material creation

keyFigures.name: Pleroma
keyFigures.role: Fullness of divine realm / the aeons

keyFigures.name: Valentinus
keyFigures.role: Founder of Valentinianism (c. 100-165 CE)

keyFigures.name: Basilides
keyFigures.role: Gnostic teacher in Alexandria

traditions.name: Manichaeism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 240 CE (Mani's first revelations)

keyFigures.name: Mani
keyFigures.role: Prophet-founder (216-274 CE), 'Seal of the Prophets'

keyFigures.name: Father of Greatness
keyFigures.role: Supreme good deity

keyFigures.name: Prince of Darkness
keyFigures.role: Evil principle

keyConcepts: particles of light trapped in matter
claim to synthesize all prior religions
Mani as final prophet after Zoroaster, Buddha, and Jesus
traditions.note: At its peak, Manichaeism was one of the most widespread religions, stretching from Rome to China

traditions.name: Hermeticism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1st-3rd century CE (Corpus Hermeticum)

keyFigures.name: Hermes Trismegistus
keyFigures.role: Legendary founder (fusion of Hermes and Thoth)

traditions.name: Greco-Buddhism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 4th century BCE to 5th century CE
keyConcepts: Greek artistic influence on Buddhist art
Milinda Panha (Greek king debates Buddhist monk)

traditions.name: Baha'i Faith
traditions.originDateLabel: 1844 CE (declaration of the Bab)

keyFigures.name: The Bab
keyFigures.role: Herald/forerunner (1819-1850)

keyFigures.name: Baha'u'llah
keyFigures.role: Founder, prophet (1817-1892)

keyFigures.name: Abdu'l-Baha
keyFigures.role: Eldest son, authorized interpreter

keyConcepts: progressive revelation (all religions from one God)

traditions.name: Navajo (Dine) Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1400 CE (Athabaskan arrival in Southwest; earlier roots in subarctic traditions dating to c. 1000 CE migration)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Dinetah region, present-day northwestern New Mexico and northeastern Arizona, USA

keyFigures.name: Changing Woman (Asdzaa Nadleehe)
keyFigures.role: Principal deity; associated with fertility, seasons, and the earth; central figure of the Blessingway ceremony

keyFigures.name: First Man (Altse Hastiin)
keyFigures.role: Primordial being who existed in the First World; helped plan the emergence into the present world

keyFigures.name: First Woman (Altse Asdzaan)
keyFigures.role: Primordial being; co-creator with First Man; associated with witchcraft and death

keyFigures.name: Monster Slayer (Naayee Neizghani)
keyFigures.role: Hero Twin; son of Changing Woman and the Sun; slayer of monsters after the Emergence

keyFigures.name: Born for Water (Tobajishchini)
keyFigures.role: Hero Twin; brother of Monster Slayer; associated with water and rain

keyFigures.name: Talking God (Haashcheeelti)
keyFigures.role: Foremost of the Diyin Dinee (Holy People); grandfather figure; leader of ceremonies

keyFigures.name: Spider Woman (Naaashjeii Asdzaan)
keyFigures.role: Wise counselor to the Hero Twins; teacher of weaving

keyConcepts: Hozho (beauty, balance, harmony — the central concept of Navajo philosophy)
Diyin Dinee (Holy People — supernatural beings who interact with humans)
Emergence myth (ascent through four underworlds to the present Fifth World)
Blessingway (Hozhoonji — foundational ceremony for maintaining harmony)
Enemyway (ceremony for protection against foreign ghosts)
Nightway (nine-night healing ceremony with Yeibichai dancers)
Sandpainting (sacred dry paintings used in healing ceremonies, destroyed after use)
traditions.evidence: Dendrochronology dates early Navajo hogans to late 15th/early 16th century. Athabaskan migration from subarctic Canada occurred c. 1000 CE, reaching the Southwest by c. 1400-1525 CE. Navajo ceremonialism shows both retained Athabaskan elements and Puebloan influences from post-1680 contact.

traditions.name: Lakota (Sioux) Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 800 CE (Woodland period Siouan culture); Plains adaptation 17th-18th century
traditions.geographicOrigin: Originally Minnesota/Wisconsin woodlands; later Great Plains, present-day Dakotas, USA

keyFigures.name: Wakan Tanka
keyFigures.role: The Great Mystery/Great Spirit — the supreme sacred power pervading all things; not a single deity but a totality of 16 sacred beings

keyFigures.name: White Buffalo Calf Woman (Ptesan Wi)
keyFigures.role: Sacred woman who brought the chanunpa (sacred pipe) and seven sacred rites to the Lakota people

keyFigures.name: Inyan
keyFigures.role: Primordial stone being; first of the Superior Wakan; created the earth from his own substance

keyFigures.name: Wi (Sun)
keyFigures.role: Chief of the Superior Wakan beings; source of light and warmth

keyFigures.name: Skan (Sky/Motion)
keyFigures.role: Spirit of motion, energy, and the sky; judge of all things

keyFigures.name: Iktomi
keyFigures.role: Trickster spider figure; culture hero and deceiver in Lakota oral tradition

keyFigures.name: Tate (Wind)
keyFigures.role: Father of the Four Winds and the Four Directions

keyConcepts: Wakan Tanka (Great Mystery — the totality of the sacred)
Seven Sacred Rites (brought by White Buffalo Calf Woman)
Wiwanyag Wachipi (Sun Dance — annual communal ceremony of sacrifice and renewal)
Hanbleciya (Vision Quest — solitary fasting for spiritual vision)
Chanunpa (Sacred Pipe — central ritual object connecting earth and sky)
Mitakuye Oyasin (All My Relations — interconnectedness of all beings)
Four Directions and Sacred Hoop (medicine wheel cosmology)
traditions.evidence: Archaeological association with Blackduck-Kathio-Clam River Continuum c. 800 CE. Siouan speakers may have originated in lower Mississippi/Ohio Valley region and participated in Mound Builder civilization (9th-12th c. CE). Seven Sacred Rites tradition attributed to White Buffalo Calf Woman 'nineteen generations ago' per oral tradition.

traditions.name: Hopi Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 CE (continuous Hopi occupation documented archaeologically); kachina tradition from c. 1100-1300 CE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Black Mesa, northeastern Arizona, USA (Hopitutskwa — Hopi ancestral lands)

keyFigures.name: Maasawu (Masau'u)
keyFigures.role: God of earth, death, fire, and the underworld; guardian of the Fourth World who granted the Hopi stewardship of the land

keyFigures.name: Tawa
keyFigures.role: Sun god and creator deity; supreme spiritual force who directed the creation of life

keyFigures.name: Spider Grandmother (Kokyangwuti)
keyFigures.role: Creator figure who helped bring humanity through the emergence; advisor and guide

keyFigures.name: Sotuknang
keyFigures.role: Nephew/servant of Tawa; executed the creation of the physical world

keyFigures.name: Eototo
keyFigures.role: Chief of all kachinas; spiritual leader who oversees the kachina ceremonies

keyFigures.name: Kokopelli
keyFigures.role: Flute-playing fertility figure; trickster and traveling musician associated with agriculture

keyConcepts: Kachinas (Katsinam — spirit beings who serve as intermediaries between humans and the divine)
Emergence myth (ascent through three underworlds to the Fourth World)
Hopi Prophecy tradition (tablets and prophecies regarding the fate of the world)
Kiva ceremonies (underground ceremonial chambers for sacred rituals)
Bean Dance (Powamuya) and Niman ceremony (kachina seasonal cycle)
Clan migration narratives (each clan's unique journey to Hopitutskwa)
Stewardship of the land (covenant with Maasawu)
Balance between the spiritual and physical worlds
traditions.evidence: Archaeological evidence documents continuous Hopi occupation since c. 500 CE. Old Oraibi settled before 1100 CE, one of the oldest continuously inhabited villages in North America. Kachina cult documented archaeologically from c. 1270 CE, with pottery at Homolovi showing kachina imagery from 1300s-1400s CE. Possible Mesoamerican influence on kachina tradition debated by scholars.

traditions.name: Inuit Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 2500 BCE (Arctic Small Tool tradition); Thule culture expansion c. 1000 CE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Arctic regions: Alaska, northern Canada, Greenland, Chukotka (Russia)

keyFigures.name: Sedna (Nuliajuk/Nerrivik)
keyFigures.role: Sea goddess and mistress of marine animals; controls access to seals, walrus, and whales; lives in the undersea realm Adlivun

keyFigures.name: Sila (Silap Inua)
keyFigures.role: Spirit of air, weather, and the atmosphere; cosmic force associated with wisdom and the breath of life

keyFigures.name: Anguta
keyFigures.role: Father of Sedna in some traditions; ferryman of the dead to the underworld

keyFigures.name: Nanook (Nanuq)
keyFigures.role: Spirit master of polar bears; governs the hunt and the relationship between hunters and bears

keyFigures.name: Tekkeitsertok
keyFigures.role: Master of caribou; deity who controls the caribou herds

keyFigures.name: Malina (Sun Woman)
keyFigures.role: Solar deity; sister of Anningan (Moon Man) in the celestial chase myth

keyConcepts: Angakkuq (shaman — mediator between humans and spirit world, healer, diviner)
Tuurngait (helping spirits or familiars acquired by angakkuq during training)
Inua (indwelling spirit or soul present in all living and natural things)
Taboo system (complex rules governing hunting, food preparation, and social behavior to maintain cosmic balance)
Soul journey (angakkuq's spirit flight to Sedna's undersea realm or the sky world)
Adlivun (underworld realm of the dead beneath the sea)
Drum dance and throat singing (ritual performance and spiritual expression)
Animal ceremonialism (rituals of respect for hunted animals to ensure their return)
traditions.evidence: Arctic Small Tool tradition (c. 2500 BCE) represents earliest Arctic cultures ancestral to Inuit. Thule culture (c. 1000 CE) spread from Alaska across Arctic Canada to Greenland, carrying shamanic traditions. Archaeological evidence of ritual practices includes miniature carvings, amulets, and drum parts from Dorset and Thule sites. Sedna myth documented across Inuit groups from Alaska to Greenland with remarkable consistency.

traditions.name: Mapuche Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE (earliest archaeological evidence of Mapuche culture in Chile)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Central-southern Chile and western Argentina (Araucania and Patagonia)

keyFigures.name: Ngenechen (Ngunechen)
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator deity; embodied in four aspects: old man, old woman, young man, young woman

keyFigures.name: Pillan
keyFigures.role: Powerful spirit associated with volcanoes, thunder, and natural forces; ancestor spirits of great chiefs

keyFigures.name: Wangulen
keyFigures.role: Star spirits; ancestral spirits who became stars

keyFigures.name: Kai Kai Filu
keyFigures.role: Serpent of the sea who causes floods; antagonist in the foundational Tren Tren myth

keyFigures.name: Tren Tren Filu
keyFigures.role: Serpent of the earth/hills who saves humanity from the flood by raising the mountains

keyFigures.name: Machi
keyFigures.role: Shaman-healer (predominantly female); mediator between human and spirit worlds, performs healing and divination through trance

keyConcepts: Ngillatun (communal prayer ceremony with offerings and animal sacrifice to Ngenechen)
Machi (shaman institution — healers, diviners, and spiritual leaders, often women)
Kultrung (sacred drum used by Machi; cosmological map painted on drumhead)
Rewe (sacred stepped altar/totem used in Machi ceremonies)
Wenu Mapu (upper/celestial world in the layered cosmology)
Tren Tren and Kai Kai (foundational flood myth of the two serpents)
Dualism of good and evil forces (Ngenechen vs. Wekufe/evil spirits)
traditions.evidence: Archaeological evidence of Mapuche culture from c. 500 BCE. Descendants of Pitren (100-1100 CE) and El Vergel (1100-1450 CE) cultures. Genetic studies show Mapuche lived in relative isolation for ~4,000 years. Language is a linguistic isolate. Ngillatun ceremony documented by early Spanish chroniclers in 16th century.

traditions.name: Guarani Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE (Tupi-Guarani cultural complex); written documentation from 16th century CE Jesuit contact
traditions.geographicOrigin: Tropical forests of Paraguay, southern Brazil, northeastern Argentina, and Bolivia

keyFigures.name: Namandu (Nande Ru)
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator deity who generated light and the cosmos from primordial darkness; 'Our Father'

keyFigures.name: Tupa
keyFigures.role: Spirit of thunder and lightning; elevated to supreme deity status during Jesuit missionization; originally subordinate to Namandu

keyFigures.name: Arasy
keyFigures.role: Moon goddess; feminine counterpart paired with Nanderuvucu in the creation

keyFigures.name: Rupave
keyFigures.role: First man created by Tupa from clay mixed with natural elements

keyFigures.name: Sypave
keyFigures.role: First woman; 'Mother of the People'; created alongside Rupave

keyFigures.name: Jasy Jatere
keyFigures.role: Spirit of the siesta; one of the seven legendary monsters born of Tau and Kerana

keyConcepts: Yvy Marane'y (Land Without Evil — utopian paradise free of death and suffering; goal of prophetic migrations)
Nee (sacred word/soul — the divine word that animates human beings)
Prophetic migration (messianic movements seeking the Land Without Evil, documented from 16th century onward)
Opy (ceremonial house — center of religious and community life)
Karai (prophet-shamans who lead spiritual movements and migrations)
Cyclical destruction and renewal of the world
Seven Legendary Monsters (children of Tau and Kerana)
Sacred songs and dances (ritual communication with the divine)
traditions.evidence: Tupi-Guarani cultural complex dates to at least 500 BCE based on linguistic reconstruction and ceramic evidence. Jesuit missionaries documented Guarani religion extensively from 1609 onward. Scholar Alfred Metraux demonstrated that Tupa was elevated from minor thunder spirit to supreme deity by missionaries. Prophetic migrations seeking Land Without Evil documented by multiple colonial sources.

traditions.name: Olmec Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500 BCE (San Lorenzo phase); major center La Venta c. 900-400 BCE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Gulf Coast lowlands of southeastern Veracruz and western Tabasco, Mexico

keyFigures.name: Were-Jaguar (Olmec Supernatural)
keyFigures.role: Human-jaguar hybrid figure with infant features; possibly the supreme deity or rain god; depicted with snarling mouth and cleft head

keyFigures.name: Maize God (God II)
keyFigures.role: Deity with maize cobs sprouting from cleft head; ancestor of later Mesoamerican maize deities; agricultural fertility

keyFigures.name: Feathered Serpent (Proto-Quetzalcoatl)
keyFigures.role: Earliest representations of the feathered serpent motif that became Quetzalcoatl in later traditions

keyFigures.name: Rain Spirit / God IV
keyFigures.role: Rain and agricultural deity with goggle eyes; precursor to later Tlaloc/Chaac traditions

keyFigures.name: Dragon/Earth Monster
keyFigures.role: Crocodilian earth deity; associated with the earth's surface and agricultural fertility

keyConcepts: Colossal stone heads (monumental portraits of rulers with possible divine status)
Jade and greenstone as sacred material (extensive jade carving for ritual use)
Mesoamerican ballgame (earliest evidence of ritual ballcourts)
Axis mundi (world tree/cosmic axis connecting underworld, earth, and sky)
Shamanic transformation (rulers as shamans mediating between worlds)
Bloodletting and sacrifice (ritual practices inherited by later cultures)
Long Count calendar origins (earliest Mesoamerican calendrical traditions)
traditions.evidence: San Lorenzo (c. 1500-900 BCE) and La Venta (c. 900-400 BCE) provide extensive archaeological evidence including colossal heads, jade figurines, votive offerings, and monumental architecture. Tate (2012) challenges were-jaguar interpretation, arguing for embryo-corn kernel iconography. Considered the 'mother culture' of Mesoamerica, though this label is debated. Olmec religious iconography clearly ancestral to Maya and Aztec traditions.

traditions.name: Mississippian Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 800 CE (emergence of Mississippian culture); Cahokia peak c. 1050-1200 CE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Mississippi River Valley; center at Cahokia (present-day Collinsville, Illinois, USA)

keyFigures.name: Morning Star (Birdman/Red Horn)
keyFigures.role: Culture hero associated with the falcon warrior; represented on the Birger Figurine and Rogan plates; associated with warfare, renewal, and the pre-dawn star

keyFigures.name: Earth Mother / Old Woman Who Never Dies
keyFigures.role: Fertility and agricultural deity associated with corn, earth, and renewal

keyFigures.name: Beneath World Serpent
keyFigures.role: Underwater/underworld serpent figure; associated with water, chaos, and the underworld

keyFigures.name: Thunderbird
keyFigures.role: Powerful sky being; eternal antagonist of the Underwater Serpent; associated with storms and celestial warfare

keyFigures.name: Corn Mother
keyFigures.role: Agricultural deity central to maize ceremonialism; source of the primary food staple

keyConcepts: Southeastern Ceremonial Complex (SECC — shared iconographic system across Mississippian sites)
Mound building (monumental earthen platforms for temples and elite residences; Monks Mound at Cahokia is largest pre-Columbian structure north of Mexico)
Falcon warrior/Birdman cult (Morning Star ideology linking warfare, gaming, and cosmic renewal)
Chunkey (sacred stone disc game with cosmological significance)
Three-world cosmology (Upper World/sky, This World/earth, Under World/water)
Woodhenge (solar calendar made of timber posts at Cahokia)
Ritual human sacrifice (documented at Mound 72, Cahokia)
traditions.evidence: Cahokia (c. 1050-1350 CE) was the largest pre-Columbian settlement north of Mexico with 10,000-20,000 inhabitants. Mound 72 excavations revealed mass burials with sacrificial victims. SECC artifacts (copper plates, shell gorgets, ceramic vessels) found from Oklahoma to Florida, dating 1200-1450 CE. Birdman tablet discovered at Cahokia. Decline by c. 1400 CE at Cahokia, but Mississippian culture persisted at other sites until European contact c. 1540-1650 CE.

traditions.name: Pacific Northwest Coast Religion (Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE (earliest coastal village sites); mature ceremonial culture by c. 500 BCE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Pacific Northwest Coast from southeastern Alaska to northern Washington; Haida Gwaii (Queen Charlotte Islands)

keyFigures.name: Raven (Yeil/Ne-kilst-lass)
keyFigures.role: Supreme trickster-creator figure who stole the sun, moon, and stars and released them to light the world; simultaneously generous benefactor and selfish deceiver

keyFigures.name: Thunderbird
keyFigures.role: Powerful sky being who creates thunder with wing beats and lightning from eyes; associated with storms and supernatural power

keyFigures.name: Dzunukwa (Wild Woman of the Woods)
keyFigures.role: Giant forest-dwelling being; both fearsome child-stealer and source of great wealth; central figure in ceremonial masks

keyFigures.name: Gunakadeit
keyFigures.role: Tlingit sea monster/wealth-bringer; associated with good fortune for those who sight it

keyFigures.name: Bakwas (Wild Man)
keyFigures.role: Ghost figure of the drowned; lures people to the land of the dead

keyFigures.name: Salmon People
keyFigures.role: Nation of beings who live in undersea villages and transform into salmon to feed humans, returning to life when bones are returned to the water

keyConcepts: Potlatch (ceremonial feast validating social rank through gift-giving, feasting, and public witnessing of rights and privileges)
Totem poles (monumental carved cedar poles recording clan lineage, mythic narratives, and hereditary crests)
Clan and moiety system (Raven/Eagle dual division organizing all social and spiritual life)
Shamanic healing (shaman's soul retrieval and spirit communication using masks and rattles)
Transformation (fluid boundaries between human, animal, and spirit forms in mythology)
Formline art (distinctive visual language encoding spiritual narratives in carved and painted art)
First Salmon ceremony (ritual honoring the first salmon catch to ensure continued abundance)
Mortuary potlatch and memorial poles (honoring the dead and transferring clan prerogatives)
traditions.evidence: Coastal village sites dating to c. 3000 BCE. Totem pole carving tradition originated with Haida and spread to Tlingit and Tsimshian. Potlatch was banned by Canadian Indian Act (1884-1951) and US authorities; revived after legal restoration. Archaeological evidence of elaborate mortuary practices, trade networks, and social stratification from 500 BCE onward. Extensive ethnographic documentation by Franz Boas and others from 1880s.

traditions.name: Taino Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 400 BCE (Arawakan migration into Caribbean from Orinoco Valley); mature Taino culture by c. 1200 CE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Greater Antilles (Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica, Puerto Rico) and The Bahamas

keyFigures.name: Yucahu Bagua Marocoti
keyFigures.role: Supreme zemi; spirit of cassava (yuca) and the sea; 'White Yuca, great and powerful as the sea and mountains'; associated with male fertility and agriculture

keyFigures.name: Atabey (Attabeira)
keyFigures.role: Mother of Yucahu; zemi of the moon, fresh waters, and female fertility; supreme feminine deity

keyFigures.name: Guabancex
keyFigures.role: Zemi of hurricanes and destruction; depicted with swirling arms representing wind and rain

keyFigures.name: Juracán
keyFigures.role: Spirit of storms; servant of Guabancex; origin of the English word 'hurricane'

keyFigures.name: Bayamanaco
keyFigures.role: Fire deity; old man figure who controlled fire and the secrets of making cassava bread

keyFigures.name: Deminán Caracaracol
keyFigures.role: Culture hero; one of four twins who stole fire and cassava from Bayamanaco; trickster figure

keyConcepts: Zemi (spirits/ancestors — both the supernatural beings and their physical representations in carved stone, wood, bone, and shell)
Cohoba ceremony (ritual inhalation of hallucinogenic snuff to communicate with zemis; preceded by fasting and purification)
Batey (ritual ballgame with cosmological significance played on rectangular courts)
Behique/Bohike (priest-healers who administered religious rites and communicated with zemis)
Cacique (chief with both political and religious authority; intermediary between people and zemis)
Areito (ceremonial dances and songs preserving oral history and honoring zemis)
Three-zone cosmology (sky world, earth/sea, and cave of origin — Cacibajagua)
Cave of origin myth (humanity emerged from caves of Cacibajagua on Hispaniola)
traditions.evidence: Arawakan-speaking Saladoid peoples migrated into Caribbean from Orinoco Delta c. 400 BCE, reaching Hispaniola by c. 250 BCE. Mature Taino culture flourished c. 1200-1500 CE. Columbus encountered Taino in 1492. Extensive Spanish documentation (Ramon Pane's 1498 'Account of the Antiquities of the Indians' is the first European ethnography of the Americas). Zemi artifacts recovered from archaeological sites across the Greater Antilles. Population devastated by disease, enslavement, and violence; largely collapsed by 1550 CE.

traditions.name: Umbanda
traditions.originDateLabel: 1908 CE (traditional founding date); formally organized 1920s-1930s in Rio de Janeiro
traditions.geographicOrigin: Niteroi and Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

keyFigures.name: Zelio Fernandino de Moraes
keyFigures.role: Founder of the first Umbanda center (1908/1920s); medium through whom Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas manifested

keyFigures.name: Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas
keyFigures.role: Indigenous spirit guide who manifested through Zelio; 'Caboclo of the Seven Crossroads'; defended inclusion of African and indigenous spirits

keyFigures.name: Oxala
keyFigures.role: Supreme orixa; syncreted with Jesus Christ; associated with creation, peace, and white

keyFigures.name: Iemanja
keyFigures.role: Orixa of the sea and motherhood; syncreted with Our Lady; protector of fishermen and children

keyFigures.name: Ogum
keyFigures.role: Orixa of war, iron, and justice; syncreted with Saint George; warrior protector

keyFigures.name: Preto Velho
keyFigures.role: Spirit category: wise old African slave spirits who offer counsel, healing, and patience

keyFigures.name: Caboclo
keyFigures.role: Spirit category: indigenous Brazilian spirits associated with forests, strength, and nature

keyConcepts: Orixas (Yoruba-derived divine beings syncreted with Catholic saints)
Mediumship (incorporation — spirits manifest through trained mediums during ceremonies)
Spiritual evolution (Kardecist concept of moral and spiritual progress through reincarnation)
Lines and Phalanges (hierarchical organization of spirit entities)
Gira (ritual session involving drumming, singing pontos, and spirit incorporation)
Pontos cantados and pontos riscados (sacred songs and drawn symbols used to invoke spirits)
Charity and spiritual healing (core ethical principle — Umbanda emphasizes service to others)
Syncretism (deliberate fusion of Yoruba orixas, Catholic saints, Kardecist spiritism, and indigenous Tupi-Guarani spirit concepts)
traditions.evidence: Traditional founding date of November 15, 1908 (Zelio de Moraes). First formal center established in Niteroi 1920s, moving to Rio de Janeiro 1938. First Umbanda Congress held in 1941 in Rio de Janeiro. Scholarship suggests multiple independent origins combining Spiritist and Afro-Brazilian practices in early 20th century. Estimated 400,000-1 million practitioners in Brazil today. UNESCO recognized Umbanda's cultural significance.

traditions.name: Dogon Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (oral tradition, Bandiagara settlement)

keyFigures.name: Amma
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god, genderless/dual-gendered, created universe from cosmic egg

keyFigures.name: Nommo
keyFigures.role: First living beings created by Amma, amphibious ancestral spirits, masters of water

keyFigures.name: Ogo/Yurugu
keyFigures.role: Rebellious twin Nommo, trickster figure who introduced disorder into creation

keyFigures.name: Lebe
keyFigures.role: First ancestor to die and be resurrected, connected to agricultural renewal

keyFigures.name: Hogon
keyFigures.role: Spiritual leader of Dogon villages, eldest male of lineage

traditions.evidence: Documented by Marcel Griaule (1930s-1950s); Bandiagara Escarpment UNESCO site; extensive ethnographic record; oral traditions maintained by Hogon priests

traditions.name: Zulu Traditional Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (Bantu migration oral traditions)

keyFigures.name: Unkulunkulu
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god who emerged from a bed of reeds, created all things

keyFigures.name: uMvelinqangi
keyFigures.role: Sky god, lord of thunder and earthquakes, sometimes identified with Unkulunkulu

keyFigures.name: Amadlozi/Amathongo
keyFigures.role: Ancestral spirits who guide, protect, and intervene in daily life

keyFigures.name: Mamlambo
keyFigures.role: River goddess associated with water and storms

keyFigures.name: Isangoma
keyFigures.role: Diviner-healers called by ancestors (ukuthwasa initiation illness)

keyFigures.name: Inyanga
keyFigures.role: Herbalist healers using natural medicine

keyConcepts: amadlozi (ancestor spirits as primary spiritual focus)
traditions.evidence: Henry Callaway ethnography (1870); living practice among 12+ million Zulu people; archaeological record of Bantu settlement in KwaZulu-Natal

traditions.name: Maasai Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (Nilotic migration oral traditions)

keyFigures.name: Enkai/Engai
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god, androgynous, manifests as Black (benevolent) and Red (wrathful)

keyFigures.name: Laibon/Laiboni
keyFigures.role: Prophet-diviners, high priests who intercede between humans and Enkai

keyFigures.name: Naiteru-kop
keyFigures.role: Mediator figure who received cattle from Enkai via the sacred wild fig tree

keyFigures.name: Olapa
keyFigures.role: Moon goddess, wife of Enkai

keyFigures.name: Le-eyo
keyFigures.role: Legendary ancestor, first Maasai, failed to properly receive the gift of immortality

keyConcepts: Enkai Narok (Black God, benevolent rain) and Enkai Nanyokie (Red God, wrathful)
cattle as divine gift from Enkai (lowered from sky)
sacred wild fig tree (connection between earth and sky)
traditions.evidence: Extensive ethnographic record from 19th century onward; living practice among 2+ million Maasai; oral traditions maintained by laiboni lineages

traditions.name: Odinani (Igbo Traditional Religion)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1000 BCE or earlier (Nri Kingdom origins, oral tradition)

keyFigures.name: Chukwu/Chineke
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god (Chi-na-eke: God who creates), source of all things

keyFigures.name: Ala/Ana
keyFigures.role: Earth goddess, enforcer of moral law, mother of all crops, most prominent deity in daily worship

keyFigures.name: Amadioha
keyFigures.role: God of thunder and lightning, justice, popular among southern Igbo

keyFigures.name: Ikenga
keyFigures.role: Horned god of fortune, industry, and personal achievement

keyFigures.name: Agwu
keyFigures.role: God of divination, healing, and sometimes madness

keyFigures.name: Ekwensu
keyFigures.role: Trickster deity of bargains and trade, later demonized by Christian missionaries

keyConcepts: chi (personal spiritual guardian assigned at birth)
traditions.evidence: Archaeological evidence from Igbo-Ukwu (9th century CE); Nri Kingdom records; extensive ethnographic literature; partially syncretized with Catholicism in modern practice

traditions.name: Kongo Religion (Bukongo)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1000 BCE or earlier (Bantu migration traditions)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Lower Congo River basin (modern DRC, Republic of Congo, Angola)

keyFigures.name: Nzambi Mpungu/Nzambi a Mpungu
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god, sky father, omnipresent sovereign

keyFigures.name: Nzambici
keyFigures.role: Female counterpart of Nzambi Mpungu, earth mother principle

keyFigures.name: Simbi
keyFigures.role: Water spirits of rivers and streams, associated with healing and divination

keyFigures.name: Nganga
keyFigures.role: Priest-healer-diviner who mediates between living and dead, prepares minkisi

keyFigures.name: Funza
keyFigures.role: Spirit of twins and sacred knowledge

keyConcepts: Yowa/dikenga cosmogram (cross-in-circle, four stages of life)
kalunga (cosmic boundary between living and dead)
ku nseke (world of the living) and ku mpemba (world of the dead)
traditions.evidence: Portuguese colonial records from 1480s onward; nkisi power figures in museums worldwide; Bakongo cosmogram documented in Robert Farris Thompson's scholarship; living practice in Central Africa

traditions.name: San/Bushman Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 30,000+ BCE (oldest continuous spiritual tradition, rock art evidence)

keyFigures.name: /Kaggen
keyFigures.role: Supreme trickster deity, manifests as praying mantis and many other forms

keyFigures.name: !Xu/Cagn
keyFigures.role: Creator god in some San groups, associated with the mantis

keyFigures.name: /Gauwa
keyFigures.role: Spirit of the dead, both helpful and harmful, associated with trickery

keyFigures.name: N/um healers
keyFigures.role: Trance dancers who activate spiritual healing energy

keyFigures.name: Eland
keyFigures.role: Sacred animal, most spiritually potent creature, central to trance dance

keyConcepts: n/um (spiritual healing energy activated through dance)
eland as most sacred and potent animal
traditions.evidence: Rock art dating 25,000+ years (Drakensberg, Tsodilo Hills); San among oldest continuous cultures on earth (~100,000 years genetic continuity); ethnographic studies by Wilhelm Bleek, Lucy Lloyd (1870s), Lorna Marshall, and Richard Lee

traditions.name: Ancient Nubian/Kushite Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 2500 BCE (Kingdom of Kerma period)

keyFigures.name: Apedemak
keyFigures.role: Lion-headed war god, protector of royalty, fertility deity, supreme Meroitic god

keyFigures.name: Amun (Nubian form)
keyFigures.role: Ram-headed god adopted from Egypt, patron of Napatan monarchy, associated with Jebel Barkal

keyFigures.name: Amesemi
keyFigures.role: Consort of Apedemak, protective goddess

keyFigures.name: Dedwen
keyFigures.role: Indigenous Nubian god of incense and wealth

keyFigures.name: Mash/Mandulis
keyFigures.role: Nubian sun god, worshipped at Kalabsha temple

keyFigures.name: Arensnuphis
keyFigures.role: Nubian companion deity, lion-headed, associated with Philae

keyConcepts: divine kingship (kandake queens and kings as earthly gods)
Amun worship at Jebel Barkal (pure/holy mountain)
traditions.evidence: Archaeological sites at Kerma, Napata, Meroe; Lion Temple at Musawwarat es-Sufra and Naqa; Meroitic script inscriptions; UNESCO World Heritage sites in Sudan

traditions.name: Waaqeffannaa (Oromo Traditional Religion)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1000 BCE or earlier (ancient Cushitic traditions)

keyFigures.name: Waaqa Tokkicha
keyFigures.role: Supreme being, omniscient and omnipotent sky god, the one God

keyFigures.name: Qaalluu/Qaallitti
keyFigures.role: High priests and ritual leaders who officiate Waaqeffannaa ceremonies

keyFigures.name: Ayyaana
keyFigures.role: Spiritual intermediaries between Waaqa and humans, guardian spirits

keyFigures.name: Abbaa Muudaa
keyFigures.role: Supreme religious authority, father of anointment, pilgrimage center

keyConcepts: Irreechaa (thanksgiving festival at water/tree sites, biannual)
Gadaa system (democratic governance with spiritual dimensions)
traditions.evidence: Living practice among ~8 million Oromo; Gadaa system recognized by UNESCO as intangible cultural heritage (2016); Irreechaa festival continues as major public event; academic studies by Lambert Bartels, Asmarom Legesse

traditions.name: Aboriginal Australian Religion (The Dreaming)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 65,000 BCE (oldest continuous religious tradition, Madjedbebe site evidence)

keyFigures.name: Rainbow Serpent
keyFigures.role: Pan-Australian creator deity, shaped waterways and landscape, giver/destroyer of life

keyFigures.name: Baiame
keyFigures.role: Sky father and creator god (southeastern Australian peoples)

keyFigures.name: Djanggawul
keyFigures.role: Three creation siblings who traveled across the land creating sacred sites (Yolngu)

keyFigures.name: Altjira
keyFigures.role: Sky god of the Arrernte people, creator of the earth then withdrew

keyFigures.name: Tiddalik
keyFigures.role: Mythological frog who drank all the water, parable of greed

keyFigures.name: Mimi spirits
keyFigures.role: Thin rock-dwelling spirits who taught humans hunting and art

keyConcepts: songlines/dreaming tracks (routes of ancestral beings sung into existence)
sacred sites and landscape as living scripture
corroboree (ceremonial gathering with dance, music, costume)
rock art as spiritual record (40,000+ year tradition)
traditions.evidence: Madjedbebe rock shelter (65,000 BP); Nawarla Gabarnmang rock art (28,000+ years); continuous oral traditions; UNESCO-listed Kakadu and Uluru; over 500 distinct language groups with shared Dreaming framework

traditions.name: Maori Religion (Nga Karakia a te Maori)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1250 CE (Polynesian settlement of Aotearoa/New Zealand)

keyFigures.name: Io (disputed)
keyFigures.role: Supreme being above all gods (debated whether pre-Christian or post-contact)

keyFigures.name: Ranginui
keyFigures.role: Sky father, locked in embrace with Papatuanuku until separated by children

keyFigures.name: Papatuanuku
keyFigures.role: Earth mother, source of all life, partner of Ranginui

keyFigures.name: Tane Mahuta
keyFigures.role: God of forests and birds, separated sky and earth, shaped first woman Hineahuone

keyFigures.name: Tangaroa
keyFigures.role: God of the sea and fish, important for seafaring culture

keyFigures.name: Tu/Tumatauenga
keyFigures.role: God of war and humanity

keyFigures.name: Maui
keyFigures.role: Demigod trickster who fished up the North Island and slowed the sun

keyConcepts: whakapapa (genealogy connecting all living things to gods)
traditions.evidence: Archaeological record of Polynesian settlement c. 1250-1300 CE; extensive oral traditions recorded by Elsdon Best, Te Rangi Hiroa (Peter Buck); living practice integrated into modern Maori culture; Treaty of Waitangi protections

traditions.name: Hawaiian Religion (Kapu System)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500-1300 CE (Polynesian settlement of Hawaii)

keyFigures.name: Kane/Kanae
keyFigures.role: God of creation, sky, sunlight, fresh water, highest of the four great gods

keyFigures.name: Ku
keyFigures.role: God of war, politics, male activities, agriculture, fishing

keyFigures.name: Lono
keyFigures.role: God of peace, fertility, agriculture, rainfall, music, celebrated during Makahiki

keyFigures.name: Kanaloa
keyFigures.role: God of the ocean, deep sea, long voyages, associated with squid/octopus

keyFigures.name: Pele
keyFigures.role: Goddess of volcanoes, fire, lightning, associated with Kilauea

keyFigures.name: Hina
keyFigures.role: Goddess of the moon, associated with feminine creative power

keyConcepts: kapu system (elaborate system of sacred taboos governing daily life)
Makahiki festival (annual harvest festival honoring Lono)
hula as sacred dance and oral tradition
traditions.evidence: Heiau temple sites across Hawaiian islands; Captain Cook's 1778 accounts; kapu system officially abolished 1819; modern Hawaiian cultural renaissance reviving traditional practices since 1970s

traditions.name: Melanesian Traditional Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 30,000+ BCE (earliest settlement of Melanesia)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Melanesia (Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Fiji)

keyFigures.name: Ancestral spirits (various)
keyFigures.role: Deceased ancestors who protect descendants, maintain order, ensure prosperity

keyFigures.name: Masalai/Bush spirits
keyFigures.role: Nature spirits inhabiting specific places, rivers, and forests

keyFigures.name: Cargo cult prophets
keyFigures.role: Charismatic leaders who predicted return of ancestors with cargo/wealth

keyFigures.name: Sorcerers/Glasman
keyFigures.role: Practitioners of magic who influence health, weather, war, and spiritual protection

keyConcepts: mana (supernatural power, can be inherited or gained through ritual)
ancestor worship (dead protect and guide the living)
sorcery and magic (influence health, weather, war outcomes)
big man system (leaders earn authority through spiritual and material generosity)
cargo cults (millenarian movements expecting ancestral return with goods)
traditions.evidence: Huon Peninsula archaeological evidence (40,000+ BP); John Frum cargo cult (Vanuatu, 1940s+); extensive ethnographic record by Bronislaw Malinowski, Margaret Mead; living practices across hundreds of distinct cultures

traditions.name: Polynesian Religion (pan-Pacific)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1000 BCE (Lapita expansion into Polynesia)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Central Pacific (Samoa, Tonga as homeland; spread throughout Polynesian triangle)

keyFigures.name: Tagaloa/Tangaroa
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god in western Polynesia, god of the sea pan-Pacific

keyFigures.name: Tane
keyFigures.role: God of forests, birds, and light; separated earth and sky in many traditions

keyFigures.name: Tu/Ku
keyFigures.role: God of war and human endeavors

keyFigures.name: Rongo/Lono
keyFigures.role: God of cultivated food, peace, agriculture, and fertility

keyFigures.name: Maui
keyFigures.role: Pan-Polynesian demigod trickster who fished up islands, slowed the sun, stole fire

keyFigures.name: Rangi and Papa
keyFigures.role: Sky father and Earth mother, primordial parents in eastern Polynesian traditions

keyConcepts: tapu/taboo (sacred restriction, things set apart from ordinary use)
noa (state of being ordinary, freed from tapu)
voyaging and navigation as sacred practice
traditions.evidence: Lapita pottery evidence (3200-2800 BP); radiocarbon dating of settlement sites across Polynesian triangle; comparative linguistics showing cognate god names; living cultural practices throughout Pacific Islands

traditions.name: Rapa Nui Religion (Easter Island)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 800-1200 CE (Polynesian settlement of Rapa Nui)

keyFigures.name: Makemake
keyFigures.role: Chief god, creator deity, embodiment of fertilizing force of nature

keyFigures.name: Hawa-tuu-take-take
keyFigures.role: Chief of the eggs, male deity associated with the birdman cult

keyFigures.name: Vie Hoa
keyFigures.role: Wife of Hawa-tuu-take-take, female deity of the birdman cult

keyFigures.name: Hotu Matu'a
keyFigures.role: Legendary first king and ancestor, led settlers to Rapa Nui

keyFigures.name: Tangata Manu
keyFigures.role: Annual birdman champion, chosen through egg-retrieval competition at Orongo

keyConcepts: moai ancestor statues (embodiments of ancestral mana)
Makemake creator god (supreme deity after moai era)
tangata manu birdman cult (annual egg-retrieval competition for rulership)
mana (spiritual power concentrated in moai and chiefs)
Orongo ceremonial village (center of birdman cult)
traditions.evidence: Moai statues (887 documented); Orongo petroglyphs with birdman imagery; rongorongo tablets; archaeological evidence of two distinct religious phases (moai era then birdman cult); ended by Christian missionaries 1860s

traditions.name: Tengrism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric steppe origins, attested by Xiongnu period c. 3rd century BCE)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Central Asian steppe (modern Mongolia, southern Siberia, Kazakhstan)

keyFigures.name: Tengri
keyFigures.role: Supreme sky god, Eternal Blue Sky, ruler of all existence

keyFigures.name: Umay
keyFigures.role: Mother goddess of fertility, virginity, and children

keyFigures.name: Erlik
keyFigures.role: God of death and the underworld, ruler of the Lower World

keyFigures.name: Genghis Khan
keyFigures.role: Proclaimed chosen of Tengri; made Tengrism state religion of the Mongol Empire

keyFigures.name: Bilge Khagan
keyFigures.role: Gokturk ruler who proclaimed Tengri as supreme deity in Orkhon inscriptions (8th century CE)

keyConcepts: Tengri (Eternal Blue Sky as supreme deity)
Three-layered cosmos (Upper World, Middle World, Lower World)
Kut (divine mandate/grace from Tengri to rulers)
Harmony with nature as life's purpose
Religious tolerance rooted in universal spiritual power
traditions.evidence: Orkhon inscriptions (8th c. CE), Herodotus accounts of Scythian sky worship, Secret History of the Mongols (13th c.), archaeological kurgans across Central Asian steppe. Modern revival movements in Tuva, Buryatia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan.

traditions.name: Bon (Yungdrung Bon)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1800 BCE (traditional dating; Zhang Zhung kingdom origins, pre-Buddhist Tibet)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Zhang Zhung kingdom (western Tibet), Olmo Lungring

keyFigures.name: Tonpa Shenrab Miwoche
keyFigures.role: Founder and supreme teacher (Buddha of Bon), born in Olmo Lungring

keyFigures.name: Shenlha Okar
keyFigures.role: Transcendent deity of white light who transmitted teachings to Tonpa Shenrab

keyFigures.name: Sidpa Gyalmo
keyFigures.role: Queen of the World, chief protectress of Bon tradition

keyFigures.name: Drenpa Namkha
keyFigures.role: 8th-century Bon master who preserved teachings during Buddhist persecution

keyConcepts: Five elements (earth, water, fire, air, space)
Zhang Zhung (ancient kingdom and sacred geography)
Dzogchen (Great Perfection meditation, shared with Nyingma Buddhism)
Three jewels: body, speech, and mind
traditions.evidence: Zhang Zhung manuscripts, Bon canonical texts (Kanjur and Tenjur), Dunhuang documents, archaeological sites in western Tibet. Menri Monastery in Dolanji (India) serves as the tradition's main exile center. Recognized as fifth spiritual tradition of Tibet by Dalai Lama.

traditions.name: Siberian Shamanism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 8000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric hunter-gatherer origins across Northern Eurasia)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Siberia (Evenki, Yakut/Sakha, Buryat, Tuvan, Khanty, Nenets territories)

keyFigures.name: Ulgen/Ulgan
keyFigures.role: Creator sky deity of the Upper World (Altai and Buryat traditions)

keyFigures.name: Erlik Khan
keyFigures.role: Lord of the dead and ruler of the Lower World

keyFigures.name: Abaasy
keyFigures.role: Spirits of the Yakut Lower World, source of disease and misfortune

keyFigures.name: Ai-Toyon
keyFigures.role: Yakut supreme deity, lord of light who created the first shaman

keyConcepts: World Tree (axis mundi connecting three cosmic realms)
Shamanic ecstatic trance via drumming and chanting
Spirit journey (soul flight to other realms)
Drum as shamanic horse or boat for spirit travel
Animal spirit helpers and totemic ancestry
traditions.evidence: Ethnographic records by Shirokogoroff (1935) on Evenki shamanism, Yakut olonkho epics (UNESCO Intangible Heritage), Buryat ritual texts, Pazyryk frozen tombs with shamanic artifacts (5th-3rd c. BCE), rock art across Siberia dating to Neolithic period.

traditions.name: Mongolian Shamanism (Boo Morgol)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 400-300 BCE (earliest attested Mongolic shamanic practices)

keyFigures.name: Qormusta Tengri
keyFigures.role: Chief deity of the 99 Tengers, supreme heavenly lord

keyFigures.name: Zayagachi
keyFigures.role: Goddess of fate who determines each person's destiny

keyFigures.name: Dayan Deerh
keyFigures.role: Powerful ancestral shaman spirit and legendary warrior

keyFigures.name: Genghis Khan
keyFigures.role: Elevated to divine ancestor spirit (sulde) after death, national shamanic figure

keyConcepts: 99 Tengers (heavenly deities: 55 western benevolent, 44 eastern malevolent)
Ongon (spirit helpers: ancestral, animal, and nature spirits)
Ovoo (sacred stone cairns as spirit dwelling places)
Hengereg (shamanic drum as vehicle for spirit travel)
Sulde (soul/spirit force, especially of warriors and khans)
Libation offerings of airag (fermented mare's milk)
Secret History of the Mongols cosmological framework
Eternal Blue Sky and Mother Earth duality
traditions.evidence: Secret History of the Mongols (1228 CE), archaeological ovoo sites, Mongolian shamanic drum collections (National Museum of Mongolia), ethnographic fieldwork by Heissig (1980) and Pegg (2001). Post-Soviet revival since 1990s with thousands of practicing shamans.

traditions.name: Scythian Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 900-700 BCE (emergence of Scythian culture in Pontic steppe)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Pontic-Caspian steppe (modern Ukraine, southern Russia, Kazakhstan)

keyFigures.name: Tabiti
keyFigures.role: Supreme goddess of fire and the hearth, most venerated deity (equated with Greek Hestia)

keyFigures.name: Papaeus
keyFigures.role: Sky father god, personification of Heaven (equated with Zeus)

keyFigures.name: Api
keyFigures.role: Earth mother goddess, consort of Papaeus

keyFigures.name: Goetosyrus/Oetosyrus
keyFigures.role: Sun god (equated with Apollo)

keyFigures.name: Argimpasa/Artimpasa
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and fertility (equated with Aphrodite Urania)

keyFigures.name: Thagimasadas
keyFigures.role: Sea/water god, worshipped by Royal Scythians (equated with Poseidon)

keyConcepts: Aniconic worship (no statues except for Ares/war-god sword altar)
Royal funerary rites with human and horse sacrifice
Enarei (androgynous shamanic seers blessed by Argimpasa)
traditions.evidence: Herodotus Histories Book IV (c. 440 BCE), Pazyryk frozen tombs with hemp-burning apparatus, gold pectoral from Tovsta Mohyla, Tillia Tepe treasure (Afghanistan), Chertomlyk vase depicting rituals. Iranian linguistic substrate confirms Indo-Iranian religious heritage.

traditions.name: Tai Folk Religion (Satsana Phi)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (prehistoric Tai animist beliefs, pre-Buddhist period)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Southeast Asian mainland (present-day Thailand, Laos, Shan State of Myanmar)

keyFigures.name: Phi Fa
keyFigures.role: Sky spirit, supreme celestial deity

keyFigures.name: Phi Muang
keyFigures.role: Guardian spirit of the city/kingdom

keyFigures.name: Phi Ban
keyFigures.role: Village guardian spirits who protect communities

keyFigures.name: Nang Kwak
keyFigures.role: Spirit of prosperity and good fortune, popular household deity

keyFigures.name: Phi Pha (or Thaen)
keyFigures.role: Heavenly deities of the Lao-Isan tradition

keyConcepts: Phi (spirits inhabiting natural features, ancestors, places)
Khwan (32 body spirits/souls that can wander from the body)
Baci/Su Khwan ceremony (ritual to recall wandering khwan)
Spirit houses (san phra phum) for guardian spirits
Maw phi (spirit doctors/mediums who communicate with phi)
Ancestral veneration and offerings at household shrines
Syncretism with Theravada Buddhism and Hindu Brahmanism
Taboos related to angering local phi spirits
traditions.evidence: Ban Chiang archaeological site (3600 BCE) with spirit-related burial practices, Lao Isan ethnographic records, Thai spirit house tradition documented since Sukhothai period (13th c. CE). Baci ceremony remains widespread across Laos and northeast Thailand.

traditions.name: Balinese Hinduism (Agama Hindu Dharma)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1st century CE (Indian Hindu-Buddhist influence arrives in Indonesian archipelago; Bali's unique form solidified by 11th century)

keyFigures.name: Sang Hyang Widhi Wasa
keyFigures.role: Supreme God, the one all-pervading divine being (Acintya)

keyFigures.name: Dang Hyang Nirartha
keyFigures.role: 16th-century Javanese Brahmin priest who reformed Balinese Hinduism

keyFigures.name: Mpu Kuturan
keyFigures.role: 11th-century sage who established the tri murti temple system in Bali

keyFigures.name: Ida Pedanda
keyFigures.role: High priest (Brahmin) lineage that maintains Vedic liturgy in Bali

keyConcepts: Tri Hita Karana (three causes of well-being: harmony with divine, humans, nature)
Canang sari (daily flower and incense offerings to spirits and gods)
Kaja-kelod (sacred directional orientation: mountain = holy, sea = impure)
Nyepi (Day of Silence, Balinese New Year)
Subak (sacred irrigation system, UNESCO World Heritage)
Sekala and Niskala (visible and invisible worlds)
Tri Murti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva as cosmic functions)
traditions.evidence: Prasasti (copper inscriptions) dating from 9th century CE in Bali, Besakih temple complex (at least 13th century), Majapahit-era literary texts (Kakawin), UNESCO inscription of subak system (2012) as embodiment of Tri Hita Karana philosophy.

traditions.name: Vietnamese Folk Religion (Dao Mau / Tu Phu)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (ancient Lac Viet animist origins; Dao Mau formalized by 16th century CE)

keyFigures.name: Lieu Hanh (Mau Lieu Hanh)

keyFigures.name: Mau Thuong Thien
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Heaven, ruler of the Heaven Palace (Thien Phu)

keyFigures.name: Mau Thoai
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Water, ruler of the Water Palace

keyFigures.name: Mau Thuong Ngan
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Mountains and Forests

keyFigures.name: Tran Hung Dao
keyFigures.role: 13th-century Vietnamese national hero, deified as powerful spirit in the Four Palaces pantheon

keyConcepts: Tu Phu (Four Palaces: Heaven, Earth, Water, Mountains/Forests)
Hau Dong/Len Dong (spirit mediumship ritual with trance possession)
Mau (Mother Goddess worship as central devotion)
Deification of historical heroes as spirits
Tam Phu (Three Palaces, earlier form of Four Palaces)
traditions.evidence: UNESCO inscription of practices related to Mother Goddess worship (2016), temple complexes in Northern Vietnam (Phu Day temple), 16th-century literary references to Lieu Hanh, ethnographic studies by Ngo Duc Thinh and others.

traditions.name: Philippine Indigenous Religion (Anitism)

traditions.geographicOrigin: Philippine archipelago (Tagalog, Visayan, Cordilleran, Lumad, and other regions)

keyFigures.name: Bathala
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god (Maykapal/Lumikha) of the Tagalog people

keyFigures.name: Laon
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator deity of the Visayan peoples, the Ancient One

keyFigures.name: Kabunyan
keyFigures.role: Creator and supreme teacher of the Cordilleran peoples (Igorot)

keyFigures.name: Babaylan
keyFigures.role: Predominantly female shamanic priestesses, spiritual leaders and healers

keyFigures.name: Manama
keyFigures.role: Highest god of the Manuvu (Bagobo) peoples of Mindanao

keyConcepts: Anito (ancestor spirits and general spirit entities)
Diwata (nature spirits and deities inhabiting trees, rivers, mountains)
Babaylan (female shamanic priestesses as spiritual intermediaries)
Pag-anito (seance ritual for communicating with ancestor spirits)
Bathala and regional supreme creator deities
Sacred groves and natural sites as spirit dwellings
traditions.evidence: Spanish colonial accounts (Boxer Codex 1590, Plasencia's Customs 1589), pre-colonial gold artifacts with deity imagery (Agusan gold image), Ifugao hudhud chants (UNESCO), living Lumad and Cordilleran traditions. Archaeological evidence from Tabon Cave (c. 47,000 BP) and Manunggul Jar (890-710 BCE).

traditions.name: Kejawen (Javanese Mysticism)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE (ancient Javanese animist roots; syncretic form developed through Hindu-Buddhist period 5th-15th c. CE and Islamic period from 15th c.)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Java, Indonesia (especially Central and East Java)

keyFigures.name: Dewi Sri
keyFigures.role: Rice goddess of fertility and prosperity, central to agricultural ritual

keyFigures.name: Sunan Kalijaga
keyFigures.role: One of the Wali Songo (Nine Saints) who synthesized Islam with Javanese mysticism

keyFigures.name: Sultan Agung
keyFigures.role: 17th-century Mataram sultan who unified Islamic and Javanese mystical calendars

keyFigures.name: Ratu Kidul
keyFigures.role: Queen of the South Sea (Indian Ocean), powerful spirit consort of Javanese kings

keyConcepts: Kebatinan (inner spiritual science, pursuit of inner harmony)
Batin (inner self/spiritual purity as highest value)
Rasa (inner feeling/intuition as path to divine union)
Slametan (communal ritual feast for spiritual harmony and life transitions)
Semedi (meditation and ascetic practice for spiritual power)
Harmony with ancestral spirits and nature spirits
traditions.evidence: Serat Centhini (19th-century Javanese encyclopedia of mysticism), Babad Tanah Jawi (chronicle of Javanese kingdoms), Clifford Geertz's Religion of Java (1960) ethnographic classification (abangan/santri/priyayi), living slametan tradition in rural Java.

traditions.name: Caodaism (Dao Cao Dai)
traditions.originDateLabel: 1926 CE (officially established in Tay Ninh, southern Vietnam)

keyFigures.name: Ngo Van Chieu
keyFigures.role: Founding medium, colonial official who first received Cao Dai revelations in seances (1920s)

keyFigures.name: Le Van Trung
keyFigures.role: First Giao Tong (Pope) of Caodaism

keyFigures.name: Pham Cong Tac
keyFigures.role: Ho Phap (spiritual protector/medium), key organizer of the Cao Dai church

keyFigures.name: Victor Hugo
keyFigures.role: Deified as a spirit saint; serves as Chief Spirit of Foreign Missions

keyFigures.name: Ly Thai Bach (Li Bai)
keyFigures.role: Deified Tang dynasty poet, serves as head spirit of Caodai spiritual hierarchy

keyConcepts: Cao Dai (Supreme Being, ultimate creator, 'High Tower')
Divine Eye (All-Seeing Eye symbol representing God's omniscience)
Third Amnesty (belief that Caodaism is God's third and final revelation to humanity)
