Hierarchical priesthood modeled on Catholic structure
Synthesis of Five Branches: Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, Christianity, Islam
traditions.evidence: Tay Ninh Holy See (constructed 1933-1955), approximately 4-6 million adherents in Vietnam (government census), Canon Codifier texts from seance sessions, recognition by Vietnamese government. Holy See is a major tourist site.

traditions.name: Muism (Korean Shamanism / Sinism)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric Korean peninsular origins, Dangun mythology dated to 2333 BCE)

keyFigures.name: Dangun
keyFigures.role: Legendary divine founder of Gojoseon (2333 BCE), son of Hwanung and bear-woman, later became Mountain God

keyFigures.name: Hwanin
keyFigures.role: Supreme celestial deity, lord of heaven, grandfather of Dangun

keyFigures.name: Hwanung
keyFigures.role: Divine prince who descended from heaven to establish sacred civilization on earth

keyFigures.name: Sanshin
keyFigures.role: Mountain God, protector of Korean land, depicted as white-bearded elder with tiger

keyFigures.name: Princess Bari
keyFigures.role: Mythological abandoned princess who journeyed to the underworld; patron saint of mudang shamans

keyConcepts: Mudang (shamanic practitioners, predominantly female, who perform gut rituals)
Gut/Kut (elaborate shamanic rituals with drumming, dance, costume changes, and spirit possession)
Sanshin (Mountain God worship at dedicated shrines on every major mountain)
Dangun mythology (divine origin of Korean nation from heavenly descent)
Sesseumu and Kangshinmu (two types of mudang: hereditary vs. spirit-called)
Samsin Halmoni (grandmother goddess of childbirth and fate)
Jeoseung Saja (psychopomp spirit who guides dead to afterlife)
Han (deep collective sorrow/unrequited grief, resolved through shamanic ritual)
traditions.evidence: Samguk Yusa (1281 CE) records Dangun myth, Yi dynasty-era mudang paintings, modern gut rituals recognized as Korean Intangible Cultural Heritage, active shrines to Sanshin throughout South Korea. Estimated 40,000-50,000 practicing mudang in contemporary South Korea.

traditions.name: Ayyavazhi
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1833 CE (incarnation of Ayya Vaikundar in Swamithope, Tamil Nadu)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Kanyakumari and Tirunelveli districts, Tamil Nadu, South India

keyFigures.name: Ayya Vaikundar
keyFigures.role: Purna avatar (full incarnation) of Narayana, founder and central figure

keyFigures.name: Narayana
keyFigures.role: Supreme God who sends Vaikundar as incarnation to destroy evil age

keyFigures.name: Kroni/Kaliyan
keyFigures.role: Primordial evil being, source of all suffering, antagonist destroyed by Vaikundar

keyFigures.name: Hari Gopalan
keyFigures.role: First disciple and key propagator of Ayyavazhi teachings

keyConcepts: Akilam (Akilathirattu Ammanai: primary scripture narrating cosmic history and Vaikundar's mission)
Arul Nool (secondary scripture with prayers, hymns, and rituals)
Dharma Yukam (age of righteousness that Vaikundar inaugurates)
Vinchai (evil force of Kali age that Vaikundar destroys)
Thuvayal Thavisu (sacred throne worship without idol images)
Thirunamam (flame-shaped symbol atop lotus, ideological emblem)
Social equality and rejection of caste discrimination
traditions.evidence: Akilathirattu Ammanai manuscript (dictated 1841), Swamithope pathi (headquarters temple), network of over 8,000 worship centers across Tamil Nadu, declared state holiday in Kanyakumari district (1994). Estimated several million adherents in southern Tamil Nadu.

traditions.name: Lingayatism (Basava Movement)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1150 CE (Basavanna's reform movement in Kalyana, Karnataka)

keyFigures.name: Basavanna (Basaveshwara)
keyFigures.role: Founder, 12th-century philosopher, poet, prime minister under King Bijjala II

keyFigures.name: Akka Mahadevi
keyFigures.role: Prominent female mystic poet, radical renunciant and spiritual teacher

keyFigures.name: Allama Prabhu
keyFigures.role: Chief mystic and president of the Anubhava Mantapa spiritual assembly

keyFigures.name: Channabasavanna
keyFigures.role: Basavanna's nephew, systematizer of Lingayat theology and practice

keyFigures.name: Siddarama
keyFigures.role: Prolific vachana poet and humanitarian who emphasized service to the poor

keyConcepts: Ishtalinga (personal Shiva linga worn on the body, symbol of formless God)
Kayaka (work is worship, dignity of all labor)
Vachana (devotional prose-poems in Kannada as scripture and spiritual practice)
Anubhava Mantapa (experiential assembly where people of all castes shared spiritual insights)
Rejection of caste system, Vedic authority, and temple idol worship
Dasoha (sharing of one's labor and wealth with the community)
traditions.evidence: Thousands of extant vachana poems in medieval Kannada, Basava Purana (13th c. hagiography), Sunya Sampadane (compilation of sharana dialogues), living Lingayat community of approximately 20 million in Karnataka. UNESCO recognized Basavanna's contributions to democratic thought.

traditions.name: Etruscan Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 900 BCE (Villanovan-Etruscan transition)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Etruria (modern Tuscany, Umbria, Lazio — central Italy)

keyFigures.name: Tinia
keyFigures.role: Supreme sky god, wielder of three thunderbolts; equivalent to Zeus/Jupiter

keyFigures.name: Uni
keyFigures.role: Queen of the gods, goddess of marriage and childbirth; equivalent to Hera/Juno

keyFigures.name: Menrva
keyFigures.role: Goddess of wisdom, arts, medicine, and warfare; equivalent to Athena/Minerva

keyFigures.name: Turan
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and fertility; equivalent to Aphrodite/Venus

keyFigures.name: Fufluns
keyFigures.role: God of wine and vegetation; equivalent to Dionysus/Bacchus

keyFigures.name: Turms
keyFigures.role: Psychopomp and messenger god; equivalent to Hermes/Mercury

keyFigures.name: Tages
keyFigures.role: Prophet child who emerged from a plowed furrow; revealed the Etrusca Disciplina

keyFigures.name: Vanth
keyFigures.role: Female demon of the underworld; guide of the dead, winged psychopomp

keyFigures.name: Charun
keyFigures.role: Underworld demon; guardian of the passage to death, carries a hammer

keyConcepts: Etrusca Disciplina (body of sacred ritual knowledge)
Piacenza Liver (bronze model dividing liver into 40 divine regions)
Libri Acherontici (books of the underworld and afterlife rituals)
fulgural divination (reading lightning as divine signs)
elaborate tomb painting depicting afterlife journeys
traditions.evidence: Tomb frescoes at Tarquinia, Piacenza Liver bronze model, temple ruins at Pyrgi with bilingual Etruscan-Phoenician gold tablets, Livy and Cicero accounts of Etruscan divination practices

traditions.name: Thracian Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500 BCE (Bronze Age Thrace)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Thrace (modern Bulgaria, northern Greece, European Turkey)

keyFigures.name: Sabazios
keyFigures.role: Supreme sky/horseman god; later syncretized with Dionysus and Jupiter Sabazius

keyFigures.name: Bendis
keyFigures.role: Goddess of the moon and hunt; equivalent to Artemis, had official cult in Athens from 430 BCE

keyFigures.name: Cotys
keyFigures.role: Goddess of fertility and wild nature; orgiastic rites involving Baptai priests

keyFigures.name: Zalmoxis
keyFigures.role: Deified prophet-god of the Getae; taught immortality of the soul and afterlife reunion

keyFigures.name: Orpheus
keyFigures.role: Legendary Thracian poet-shaman; founder of Orphic mysteries, descended to underworld

keyFigures.name: The Thracian Horseman
keyFigures.role: Anonymous rider deity depicted on thousands of votive reliefs; divine hunter-hero

keyConcepts: Thracian Horseman cult (hero-rider reliefs found across Balkans)
vegetarianism and asceticism in Orphic path
dual spiritual paths: Orphic ascent vs. Sabazian descent
human sacrifice and messenger-sending to Zalmoxis (Herodotus 4.94)
traditions.evidence: Herodotus Histories Book 4, thousands of Thracian Horseman reliefs, Thracian gold treasures (Panagyurishte, Rogozen), Seuthopolis temple complex, Derveni krater with Dionysiac scenes

traditions.name: Iberian and Celtiberian Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1000 BCE (Iron Age Iberia)

keyFigures.name: Endovelicus
keyFigures.role: Supreme Lusitanian healing and oracular god; 94 known dedications, most venerated deity in western Iberia

keyFigures.name: Ataecina
keyFigures.role: Underworld and regeneration goddess; Turobrigensis epithet, nearly 40 inscriptions, equated with Proserpina

keyFigures.name: Bandua
keyFigures.role: Protector deity of communities and territories; multiple epithets across Lusitania and Gallaecia

keyFigures.name: Nabia
keyFigures.role: Goddess of rivers and water; widespread cult in Gallaecia and northern Lusitania

keyFigures.name: Lugus
keyFigures.role: Celtic astral god; worshipped at Penalba de Villastar sanctuary, equated with Mercury

keyFigures.name: Cosus
keyFigures.role: War god of the Gallaeci and Lusitani; widespread epigraphic attestation

keyConcepts: oracular healing sanctuaries (especially Endovelicus at Sao Miguel da Mota)
devotio iberica (ritual self-sacrifice of warriors bound to a leader)
community-protecting federative deities with territorial epithets
animal sacrifice (pigs, boars, birds) and votive offerings
traditions.evidence: Hundreds of Latin-alphabet votive inscriptions, sanctuary of El Trampal (Alcuescar), Penalba de Villastar rock inscriptions, Lusitanian language inscriptions at Cabeço das Fraguas and Lamas de Moledo

traditions.name: Minoan Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE (Early Minoan period)

keyFigures.name: Great Mother Goddess
keyFigures.role: Supreme fertility and earth goddess; mountain goddess worshipped at peak sanctuaries

keyFigures.name: Snake Goddess
keyFigures.role: Household protectress, chthonic deity; faience figurines found at Knossos Temple Repositories (1903)

keyFigures.name: Potnia Theron
keyFigures.role: Mistress of Animals; nature goddess depicted flanked by wild beasts

keyFigures.name: Bull God
keyFigures.role: Sacred masculine divinity connected to bull-leaping ritual; consort of the goddess

keyFigures.name: Dove Goddess
keyFigures.role: Aspect of the mother goddess associated with peace and the heavens

keyFigures.name: Britomartis
keyFigures.role: Cretan mountain and hunting goddess; later absorbed into Artemis cult

keyConcepts: palace-temple complexes as ritual centers (Knossos, Phaistos, Malia)
bull-leaping ritual (taurokathapsia) as central religious ceremony
snake cult and chthonic goddess worship
double axe (labrys) as primary sacred symbol
labyrinth mythology (possible origin of Minotaur legend)
traditions.evidence: Knossos palace frescoes, faience snake goddess figurines, Phaistos Disc, Agia Triada sarcophagus depicting ritual scenes, peak sanctuary votive deposits, sacred cave finds at Psychro and Kamares

traditions.name: Mycenaean Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1600 BCE (Shaft Grave period)

keyFigures.name: Di-wo (Zeus)
keyFigures.role: Sky father deity; attested in Linear B tablets at Knossos and Pylos

keyFigures.name: Po-se-da-o (Poseidon)
keyFigures.role: Major deity, possibly supreme at Pylos; earth-shaker, horse god

keyFigures.name: Di-wo-nu-so (Dionysus)
keyFigures.role: Attested in Linear B; interpreted as 'son of Zeus,' earliest known reference

keyFigures.name: A-te-mi-to (Artemis)
keyFigures.role: Huntress goddess; earliest attested form in Mycenaean Greek at Pylos

keyFigures.name: E-ma-a2 (Hermes)
keyFigures.role: Messenger deity; attested in Linear B tablets

keyFigures.name: Potnia
keyFigures.role: The Lady/Mistress; generic title for major goddesses, possibly applied to multiple deities

keyFigures.name: Wanax
keyFigures.role: The king as semi-divine religious authority overseeing palace cult and sacrifices

keyConcepts: wanax (king) as central religious and political authority
Linear B tablet records of offerings and sacrifice schedules
proto-Olympian pantheon (Zeus, Poseidon, Dionysus, Artemis, Hermes attested)
palatial cult centers with organized priesthood hierarchy
priestess of Pakijane, hieroworgos (sacrificial priest), sphageus (sacrificer)
continuity with later Classical Greek religion
traditions.evidence: Linear B tablets from Pylos, Knossos, Thebes; Cult Center at Mycenae; shrine complexes; shaft grave burial goods; fresco fragments depicting ritual processions

traditions.name: Basque Mythology
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (pre-Indo-European survival)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Basque Country (Euskal Herria — modern Basque regions of Spain and France)

keyFigures.name: Mari
keyFigures.role: Supreme earth goddess; personification of Mother Earth, dwells in caves and mountaintops, controls weather

keyFigures.name: Sugaar
keyFigures.role: Storm god and serpent/dragon deity; consort of Mari, appears as fireball or serpent crossing the sky

keyFigures.name: Akerbeltz
keyFigures.role: Black goat spirit; protector of animals, dual nature — benevolent guardian and president of akelarres (witch assemblies)

keyFigures.name: Jentilak
keyFigures.role: Race of pre-Christian giants; immensely strong, built dolmens and cromlechs, fled with arrival of Christianity

keyFigures.name: Basajaun
keyFigures.role: Lord of the Forest; wild man and protector of flocks, taught agriculture and ironworking to humans

keyFigures.name: Gaueko
keyFigures.role: Lord of the Night; spirit of darkness who punishes those who violate nighttime prohibitions

keyConcepts: chthonic religion (all beings dwell on earth or below, sky as empty corridor)
matriarchal religious structure centered on Mari
akelarre (witches' sabbath gatherings associated with Akerbeltz)
cave sanctuaries as primary sacred spaces
animistic landscape — every mountain, river, and cave has its spirit
traditions.evidence: Oral folk traditions collected by Jose Miguel de Barandiaran and others, megalithic monuments (dolmens, cromlechs), cave sanctuaries, place-name evidence, Basque language itself as pre-IE survival

traditions.name: Finnish and Sami Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500 BCE (Finno-Ugric traditions)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Finland, Sami regions of northern Scandinavia and Kola Peninsula

keyFigures.name: Ukko
keyFigures.role: Supreme sky and thunder god; wielder of hammer/axe, god of weather and crops, replaced earlier sky god Jumala

keyFigures.name: Väinämöinen
keyFigures.role: Primordial shaman-hero; greatest singer and sage, born of Ilmatar, central figure of the Kalevala

keyFigures.name: Ilmarinen
keyFigures.role: Eternal smith, former sky god; forged the Sampo (world-pillar/cosmic mill) and the dome of the sky

keyFigures.name: Lemminkäinen
keyFigures.role: Reckless hero-shaman; dies and is reassembled by his mother, underworld journeyer

keyFigures.name: Tapio
keyFigures.role: Forest god; ruler of the woodland realm Tapiola, master of game animals

keyFigures.name: Ahti
keyFigures.role: Water god; ruler of seas, lakes, and fish

keyFigures.name: Tuoni
keyFigures.role: God of the dead; ruler of Tuonela, the Finnish underworld

keyFigures.name: Noaidi (Sami shaman)
keyFigures.role: Sami religious specialist; mediator between human world and saivo (spirit world) using drum and joik chanting

keyConcepts: noaidi shamanism and Sami drum (runebomme) ecstatic trance journeys
joik chanting as sacred vocal tradition for contacting spirits
Sampo as cosmic axis/world-pillar/mill of abundance
Kalevala as 19th-century compilation of pre-Christian oral tradition by Elias Lönnrot
haltija (nature spirits inhabiting every natural feature)
sieidi (Sami sacred stones and natural cult sites)
traditions.evidence: Kalevala rune poems collected by Lönnrot, Sami drums in museum collections, Mikael Agricola's 1551 list of Finnish deities, Christfrid Ganander's Mythologia Fennica (1789), archaeological sieidi sites across Sapmi

traditions.name: Eleusinian Mysteries
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500 BCE (Mycenaean origins at Eleusis)

keyFigures.name: Demeter
keyFigures.role: Goddess of grain and agriculture; the mourning mother whose grief caused the seasons

keyFigures.name: Persephone (Kore)
keyFigures.role: Queen of the underworld; daughter of Demeter, her annual return symbolizes rebirth

keyFigures.name: Hades/Plouton
keyFigures.role: Lord of the underworld who abducted Persephone; giver of wealth from below

keyFigures.name: Triptolemus
keyFigures.role: Culture hero taught agriculture by Demeter; sent to spread grain cultivation to humanity

keyFigures.name: Iacchus
keyFigures.role: Deity of the ritual procession from Athens to Eleusis; torch-bearer, associated with Dionysus

keyFigures.name: Hierophant
keyFigures.role: High priest of the Eumolpidae family who revealed sacred objects in the Telesterion

keyConcepts: kykeon (sacred barley and pennyroyal drink — debated psychotropic properties)
three stages of initiation: descent (loss), search, ascent (reunion/rebirth)
Lesser Mysteries (Myesis — spring purification at Agrae)
Greater Mysteries (Epopteia — autumn initiation at Eleusis)
Telesterion (great hall of initiation where sacred objects were revealed)
hiera (sacred objects revealed only to initiates — contents never disclosed)
promise of blessed afterlife for initiates — freedom from fear of death
strict secrecy enforced on pain of death
traditions.evidence: Homeric Hymn to Demeter, Telesterion archaeological remains at Eleusis, Plutarch and Cicero testimonies, Eleusinian relief sculptures, Ninnion Tablet (votive offering showing initiation scenes)

traditions.name: Orphic Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 6th century BCE (earliest Orphic poetry)

keyFigures.name: Orpheus
keyFigures.role: Legendary Thracian founder; supreme poet-musician who descended to Hades to retrieve Eurydice

keyFigures.name: Zagreus
keyFigures.role: First Dionysus; son of Zeus and Persephone, dismembered by Titans, his heart saved by Athena

keyFigures.name: Dionysus (Orphic)
keyFigures.role: Reborn Zagreus; second incarnation from Zeus and Semele, liberator of the divine spark in humanity

keyFigures.name: Phanes/Protogonos
keyFigures.role: Orphic primordial god; born from cosmic egg, hermaphroditic first being, source of all creation

keyFigures.name: Persephone (Orphic)
keyFigures.role: Queen of the underworld; mother of Zagreus, judge of souls in the afterlife

keyConcepts: metempsychosis (transmigration of souls through multiple lives)
Zagreus myth (Titans devour divine child — humanity born from Titan ash containing divine spark)
soma-sema (body as tomb/prison of the divine soul)
Orphic gold tablets (afterlife instructions: avoid Lethe, drink from Mnemosyne's pool)
Orphic theogony (cosmic egg, Phanes, alternative creation narrative)
ascetic lifestyle: vegetarianism, celibacy, purification rituals
wheel of rebirth (soul reincarnates 10 times before release)
Derveni Papyrus (oldest European manuscript, 5th c. BCE, contains Orphic theogony commentary)
traditions.evidence: Derveni Papyrus (c. 340 BCE), Orphic gold tablets found at Thurii, Pelinna, Hipponion, Petelia; Orphic Hymns collection; Plato's references in Phaedo, Republic, Cratylus; bone tablets from Olbia inscribed 'Life-Death-Life'

traditions.name: Mithraic Mysteries
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 80 CE (earliest archaeological evidence in Rome)

keyFigures.name: Mithras
keyFigures.role: Central deity; cosmic bull-slayer, rock-born (petra genetrix), savior god

keyFigures.name: Sol (Helios)
keyFigures.role: Sun god; ally and banquet companion of Mithras, Sol Invictus connection

keyFigures.name: Cautes
keyFigures.role: Torch-bearer with upraised torch; symbolizes dawn/spring/life

keyFigures.name: Cautopates
keyFigures.role: Torch-bearer with lowered torch; symbolizes dusk/autumn/death

keyFigures.name: Pater (Father)
keyFigures.role: Highest grade of initiate; leader of the mithraeum community, representative of Saturn

keyConcepts: tauroctony (ritual bull-killing scene — central icon in every mithraeum)
seven grades of initiation: Corax (Raven), Nymphus (Bridegroom), Miles (Soldier), Leo (Lion), Perses (Persian), Heliodromus (Sun-Runner), Pater (Father)
mithraeum (cave-temple replicating cosmic cave, vaulted ceiling with stars)
communal ritual meal (bread and water/wine shared among initiates)
Sol Invictus Mithras identification (Mithras as unconquered sun)
exclusively male membership, especially popular among soldiers
astrological symbolism (zodiac, planetary associations with grades)
traditions.evidence: Over 400 mithraea excavated across the Roman Empire, T. Flavius Hyginus inscription (80-100 CE), Santa Prisca mithraeum frescoes and inscriptions, Dura-Europos mithraeum, CIMRM (Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae) catalog

traditions.name: Isis Mystery Cult
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 300 BCE (Ptolemaic formalization of Hellenized Isis cult)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Ptolemaic Egypt, spreading across Greco-Roman Mediterranean

keyFigures.name: Isis
keyFigures.role: Supreme goddess; universal mother, mistress of magic, protector of sailors (Isis Pelagia), healer

keyFigures.name: Osiris
keyFigures.role: Dying-and-rising god; lord of the afterlife, resurrected husband of Isis

keyFigures.name: Serapis
keyFigures.role: Syncretic Ptolemaic deity combining Osiris and Apis; male consort of Isis in Hellenized cult

keyFigures.name: Horus/Harpocrates
keyFigures.role: Divine child of Isis and Osiris; depicted as child with finger to lips

keyFigures.name: Anubis
keyFigures.role: Jackal-headed guide of the dead; psychopomp in the Isiac mysteries

keyFigures.name: Lucius (Apuleius' character)
keyFigures.role: Protagonist of The Golden Ass; only narrative account of Isiac initiation (2nd c. CE)

keyConcepts: Isis aretalogy (self-proclamations of Isis as supreme universal deity)
Isiac initiation (purifying baths, fasting, donning linen, visionary experience of death and rebirth)
Isis Pelagia (mistress of the sea, protector of sailors and navigation)
Navigium Isidis (March 5 festival opening the sailing season with ship procession)
Isis as myrionyme (goddess of ten thousand names — universal feminine deity)
Serapis cult as Ptolemaic state-sponsored syncretism
dream incubation (Isis appears in dreams to choose initiates)
daily temple liturgy (morning awakening of the goddess, evening closing)
traditions.evidence: Apuleius Metamorphoses Book 11, Iseum at Pompeii, Isis temple at Philae (last pagan temple closed 535 CE), hundreds of Isis statuettes across Roman Empire, Oxyrhynchus Isis hymn, Alexandrian Isis coins

traditions.name: Neoplatonism (as Religious Practice)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 245 CE (Plotinus begins teaching in Rome)

keyFigures.name: Plotinus
keyFigures.role: Founder; philosopher-mystic who taught henosis (union with the One) through intellectual contemplation (204-270 CE)

keyFigures.name: Porphyry
keyFigures.role: Student of Plotinus; editor of Enneads, advocate of philosophical asceticism, critic of Christianity

keyFigures.name: Iamblichus
keyFigures.role: Systematizer of theurgy; added ritual practice to philosophical contemplation, hierarchies of divine beings (c.245-325 CE)

keyFigures.name: Proclus
keyFigures.role: Last great Athenian Neoplatonist; synthesized all previous Neoplatonism, influenced Islamic and Christian theology (412-485 CE)

keyFigures.name: Emperor Julian (the Apostate)
keyFigures.role: Roman emperor who attempted to restore pagan Neoplatonic religion against Christianity (r. 361-363 CE)

keyFigures.name: Hypatia
keyFigures.role: Alexandrian Neoplatonist philosopher and teacher; murdered by Christian mob in 415 CE

keyConcepts: The One (To Hen) — transcendent first principle beyond all being and thought
henosis (mystical union with the One as ultimate goal)
emanation (reality flows from the One through Nous/Intellect, Soul, to Matter)
theurgy (divine ritual work to ascend through cosmic hierarchy back to the One)
divine hierarchy: the One > Nous (Intellect/Divine Mind) > Psyche (World Soul) > Matter
Chaldean Oracles as theurgic scripture (used by Iamblichus and later Neoplatonists)
return of the soul (epistrophe) through purification and contemplation
influence on Christian theology (Pseudo-Dionysius, Augustine, Cappadocian Fathers)
traditions.evidence: Plotinus' Enneads (edited by Porphyry), Iamblichus' De Mysteriis, Proclus' Elements of Theology and Platonic Theology, Julian's Hymn to King Helios, closure of Athenian Academy by Justinian in 529 CE

traditions.name: Catharism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1140 CE (first documented Cathar activity in Languedoc)

keyFigures.name: Nicetas (Niquinta)
keyFigures.role: Bogomil bishop who presided over the Cathar Council of Saint-Felix (1167 CE), establishing Cathar dioceses

keyFigures.name: Guilhabert de Castres
keyFigures.role: Cathar bishop of Toulouse; prominent leader during the Albigensian Crusade period

keyFigures.name: Esclarmonde de Foix
keyFigures.role: Noblewoman who became a perfecta; defended Catharism, patron of Montsegur

keyFigures.name: Guillaume Belibaste
keyFigures.role: Last known Cathar perfectus; executed in 1321 CE, marking near-extinction of the faith

keyConcepts: radical dualism (good God of spirit vs. evil demiurge of matter — two opposing creators)
perfecti (the Perfect — spiritual elite who had received the consolamentum)
credentes (ordinary believers who had not yet received full baptism)
consolamentum (spiritual baptism by laying on of hands — only sacrament, could be deathbed rite)
rejection of the material world, physical body, and Old Testament God as evil
strict asceticism of perfecti: vegetarianism, celibacy, poverty, refusal of oaths
endura (ritual fasting unto death practiced by some devout followers)
Bogomil origins (theological lineage from Bulgarian dualist movement)
traditions.evidence: Inquisition records (especially Bernard Gui and Jacques Fournier), surviving Cathar texts (Liber de Duobus Principiis, Rituel Cathare), archaeological remains at Montsegur, Council of Saint-Felix records (1167), Albigensian Crusade chronicles

traditions.name: Kabbalah
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1150 CE (Sefer ha-Bahir circulates in Provence)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Provence (southern France) and Catalonia (Spain), building on earlier Merkabah mysticism

keyFigures.name: Isaac the Blind
keyFigures.role: Provençal mystic (c.1160-1235); earliest known Kabbalist, teacher of the Gerona circle

keyFigures.name: Moses de Leon
keyFigures.role: Likely author/compiler of the Zohar (c.1286), attributed it to 2nd-century Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai

keyFigures.name: Abraham Abulafia
keyFigures.role: Ecstatic Kabbalist (1240-1291); developed prophetic/meditative Kabbalah using letter permutations

keyFigures.name: Isaac Luria (Ha-Ari)
keyFigures.role: Father of modern Kabbalah (1534-1572); developed doctrines of tzimtzum, shevirat ha-kelim, tikkun olam in Safed

keyFigures.name: Moses Cordovero
keyFigures.role: Systematic Kabbalist (1522-1570); wrote Pardes Rimonim, systematized Kabbalistic thought before Luria

keyFigures.name: Shimon bar Yochai
keyFigures.role: 2nd-century Talmudic sage; traditional attributed author of the Zohar

keyConcepts: Ein Sof (the Infinite — unknowable divine essence before creation)
sefirot (ten divine emanations/attributes through which Ein Sof interacts with creation)
Tree of Life (Etz Chaim — diagram mapping the ten sefirot and 22 connecting paths)
Zohar (foundational Kabbalistic text, Aramaic, compiled c. 1286)
tzimtzum (divine contraction/withdrawal to create space for the world — Lurianic doctrine)
shevirat ha-kelim (shattering of the vessels — cosmic catastrophe scattering divine sparks)
tikkun olam (repair of the world by gathering scattered divine sparks through righteous action)
traditions.evidence: Sefer ha-Bahir (c.1150), Zohar manuscripts (c.1286), Sefer Yetzirah (early roots, c. 3rd-6th century), Isaac Luria's teachings recorded by Hayyim Vital, Lurianic prayer books, influence on Hasidic movement from 18th century onwards

traditions.name: Druze Faith
traditions.originDateLabel: 1017 CE (Hamza ibn Ali proclaims divine unity of al-Hakim)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Fatimid Cairo, Egypt; spread to Levantine mountains

keyFigures.name: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah
keyFigures.role: Fatimid Caliph (985-1021 CE); believed by Druze to be the incarnation of God in human form

keyFigures.name: Hamza ibn Ali
keyFigures.role: Founder and primary theologian; proclaimed al-Hakim's divinity, considered reincarnation of Christ and Universal Mind (al-Aql)

keyFigures.name: Baha al-Din al-Muqtana
keyFigures.role: Second-ranking minister; authored many of the Epistles of Wisdom (Rasa'il al-Hikma)

keyFigures.name: Jethro (Shu'ayb)

connections.evidence: Linguistic cognates (Dyaus=Dyeus), shared rituals, Rigveda hymns
Zeus from *Dyeus, cognate deities, shared mythological motifs
Jupiter from *Dyeus Pater, Italic branch of IE
Thor from *Perkwunos, Tyr from *Dyeus, runic traditions
Taranis cognate with thunder god, Lugus pan-Celtic
Perun from *Perkwunos, Veles from *Welnos
Dievas from *Dyeus, Perkunas from *Perkwunos, Saule from *Seh2ul; most conservative IE branch
Indo-Iranian branch; Ahura/Asura cognate, Mithra shared with Vedic Mitra
El-Yahweh merger, shared vocabulary, Ugaritic parallels in Hebrew Bible
Babylonian Exile catalyzed full monotheism
Angels/demons, eschatology, resurrection, Satan figure, heaven/hell dualism
Buddha rejected Vedic ritual and caste but shared cosmological concepts
Sufi mysticism influenced Guru Nanak's monotheistic vision
Interpretatio graeca/romana, wholesale adoption of Greek myths
Alexander's conquests, Greek art styles in Gandhara
Isis cult adopted by Greeks, Serapis creation, Herodotus correspondences
Hermes Trismegistus = Hermes + Thoth fusion
Silk Road transmission, Chan/Pure Land/Tiantai schools developed
Shinbutsu-shugo syncretism dominated Japanese religion for over 1000 years

connections.note: Also major Fon/Dahomey and Kongo influences

connections.evidence: Eschatology, angels/demons, resurrection, final judgment, Magi narrative
Shared Mesoamerican cosmology, Quetzalcoatl/Kukulkan, calendar systems
Neoplatonic concepts shaped Christian theology (Augustine, Pseudo-Dionysius)

Olmec religious iconography (feathered serpent, maize god, rain deity, cosmic tree, ballgame) directly ancestral to Classic Maya religion. Shared Long Count calendar tradition. La Venta Olmec site overlaps geographically with early Maya zones.

Olmec feathered serpent became Quetzalcoatl; Olmec rain deity became Tlaloc; Olmec maize god became Centeotl. Transmission through intermediary Mesoamerican cultures (Teotihuacan, Toltec). Shared cosmological framework of multiple world ages.

Shared Mesoamerican religious complex: feathered serpent (Kukulkan/Quetzalcoatl), rain deities (Chaac/Tlaloc), ballgame, calendrical system, human sacrifice, multi-layered cosmology. Parallel rather than linear influence in late period.

Some scholars propose Mesoamerican origins for Hopi kachina cult based on iconographic parallels. Macaw feathers and copper bells from Mesoamerica found at Pueblo sites. However, many ethnologists argue the kachina tradition arose organically in the Southwest. Debated.

Navajo adopted significant Puebloan religious elements after arrival in the Southwest (c. 1400-1500 CE). After the Pueblo Revolt of 1680, Pueblo refugees lived among Navajo, accelerating cultural exchange. Navajo sandpainting, kachina-like Yeibichai figures, and agricultural ceremonies show strong Puebloan influence.

Siouan speakers may have participated in Mound Builder civilization (9th-12th c. CE). Shared cosmological elements: three-world cosmology (sky, earth, water), Thunderbird vs. Underwater Serpent opposition, Morning Star significance, chunkey game. Lakota may carry vestigial Mississippian religious elements.

Haitian Vodou developed on Hispaniola where Taino religion had been practiced. Some scholars identify possible Taino substrate influences in Vodou, including zemi concepts paralleling lwa and shared Caribbean landscape spirits. However, primary Vodou sources are West African (Fon/Ewe/Yoruba) traditions.

Umbanda directly incorporates Candomble's orixa system (Oxala, Iemanja, Ogum, etc.) and much of its ritual vocabulary. However, Umbanda simplifies and universalizes Candomble elements, adding Kardecist spiritism and indigenous spirit categories (Caboclos, Pretos Velhos).

Umbanda's Caboclo spirit category draws on romanticized indigenous (particularly Tupi-Guarani) spiritual concepts. Forest spirits, indigenous nature reverence, and healing practices inform Umbanda's indigenous strand. Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas, the founding spirit, self-identified as indigenous.

Both Santeria and Umbanda derive orixa/orisha systems from Yoruba tradition and practice Catholic syncretism. Shared Yoruba theological substrate though independently developed in Cuba vs. Brazil. Cross-pollination increased with modern media and travel.

Both are African-diaspora syncretic religions with spirit possession/incorporation as central practice. Shared West African religious substrate (Fon/Ewe lwa parallel Yoruba orixas). Different colonial contexts (French Haiti vs. Portuguese Brazil) produced distinct but structurally parallel systems.

Both traditions share broad shamanic and animistic frameworks common to many indigenous North American religions: vision quests, animal spirit relationships, cosmic dualism. These likely reflect deep shared ancestry rather than direct contact. Trickster figures (Iktomi/various Inuit tricksters) show structural parallels.

Inca Empire expanded to edge of Mapuche territory in the 15th century. Limited Inca influence on northern Mapuche groups. Mapuche successfully resisted Inca conquest, limiting religious exchange. Some scholars note solar/agricultural ceremonial parallels but direct borrowing is debated.

Both traditions share Thunderbird mythology, elaborate mortuary practices, and complex ranked societies. Likely reflects deep shared Native American cosmological substrate rather than direct contact. Both developed sophisticated art traditions encoding spiritual narratives.
Shared West African religious substrate; both feature supreme god with spirit intermediaries, divination systems, and trickster figures; geographic proximity in Nigeria
Enslaved Bakongo people brought Kongo religious concepts to Haiti; Bakongo cosmogram, nkisi concepts, and Simbi water spirits directly influenced Vodou lwa system
Large Bantu-speaking populations enslaved in Brazil brought Kongo religious practices; Candomble Angola/Congo nation preserves Kongo elements alongside Yoruba
Enslaved Igbo people in Haiti contributed elements to Vodou; Igbo religious concepts merged into broader African diaspora religious matrix
Igbo religious elements contributed to Cuban syncretic traditions alongside dominant Yoruba framework

Nubian adoption of Amun worship, pyramid burial practices, and Egyptian temple architecture; Kushites ruled Egypt as 25th Dynasty (747-656 BCE); later Meroitic period developed indigenous Apedemak cult partially replacing Egyptian deities
Egyptians worshipped Nubian deities Dedwen, Bes, Menhit, and Mandulis; bidirectional religious exchange along Nile corridor
Maori are Polynesian settlers who brought pan-Polynesian gods (Tangaroa, Tane, Tu, Rongo) and concepts (mana, tapu, marae) to Aotearoa, adapting them to new environment
Hawaiian religion derives from Polynesian settlers (Marquesas, Tahiti); cognate gods Kane=Tane, Ku=Tu, Lono=Rongo, Kanaloa=Tangaroa; kapu system from Tahitian influence c. 1200 CE

Rapa Nui settlers came from eastern Polynesia; Makemake shares traits with Polynesian creator gods; mana and tapu concepts preserved; moai represent Polynesian ancestor veneration taken to extreme form
Lapita peoples passed through Melanesia en route to Polynesia; mana concept shared between Melanesian and Polynesian traditions; ancestral substrate influences
West African religious substrate shared between Niger-Congo peoples; some scholars note parallels between Yoruba Olodumare and Dogon Amma as withdrawn supreme creators; trade along Niger River
Both derive from Bantu migration religious substrate; shared ancestor worship emphasis, supreme creator concept, and diviner-healer traditions common to Bantu-speaking peoples

San were the original inhabitants of Southern Africa before Bantu migrations; Zulu adopted some San spiritual elements including trance healing practices and certain rock art motifs; archaeological evidence of cultural contact
Zionist Christian churches blend Zulu ancestor veneration with Christianity; isangoma traditions persist alongside Christian practice; syncretic Shembe church (Nazareth Baptist Church)
Islam spread in Mali from 11th century; many Dogon have adopted Islamic practices while maintaining traditional ceremonies; syncretic coexistence in Bandiagara region

Christian missionaries (CMS from 1857) heavily impacted Igbo religion; Odinani largely syncretized with Catholicism; traditional practices persist in rural areas; Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart documents this transition
American Protestant missionaries arrived 1820; kapu system already abolished 1819; rapid conversion followed; Hawaiian cultural renaissance since 1970s has revived traditional practices

Scythians were Iranian-speaking Indo-Europeans; their deities (Tabiti, Papaeus) derive from Proto-Indo-Iranian divine concepts. Linguistic analysis confirms Iranian etymologies for Scythian divine names.
Sky-father worship and tripartite cosmos in Tengrism show possible Indo-European substrate influence. Tengri concept may share deep roots with PIE *Dyeus, though Turkic-Mongolic origins are primary.
Both traditions share Indo-Iranian roots. Papaeus parallels Ahura Mazda, Tabiti parallels fire worship (Atar). Herodotus noted similarities. Scythians interacted with Persians extensively.
Tengrism developed from prehistoric Siberian shamanic substrate. World-tree cosmology, shamanic ecstasy, spirit-helper concepts, and sky-worship all derive from broader Siberian shamanic traditions.
Mongolian shamanism shares world-tree cosmology, drum-journeying, and spirit-helper traditions with broader Siberian shamanism. Buryat shamanism bridges the two traditions geographically.
Mongolian shamanism (Boo Morgol) incorporated Tengri as chief deity with 99 Tengers. Secret History of the Mongols records Tengri worship as central. The two traditions are deeply intertwined.

Bon and Tibetan Buddhism heavily influenced each other from 7th century CE onward. Dzogchen tradition is shared. Bon adopted Buddhist monastic structures; Buddhism absorbed Bon protective deities and rituals.

Tibetan Buddhism was adopted by Mongol khans from 13th century; by 16th century under Altan Khan, Buddhism became dominant while absorbing shamanic elements. Many shamanic spirits were reinterpreted as Buddhist protectors.

Korean shamanism shares Siberian shamanic features: ecstatic trance, spirit possession, world-tree cosmology, drum usage, bear mythology. Dangun bear-woman myth parallels Siberian bear cults. Migration routes via Manchuria.

Muism shares core Siberian shamanic elements: ecstatic spirit possession, bear mythology (Dangun myth), mountain god veneration, cosmological three-world structure. Korean peninsula was a southern terminus of Siberian shamanic diffusion.

Theravada Buddhism integrated with Tai animism from 13th century Sukhothai period onward. Buddhist temples coexist with spirit houses. Monks perform merit-making rituals alongside phi spirit ceremonies.
Hindu Brahmanical elements entered Tai religion through Khmer and Mon intermediaries. Royal court rituals, Brahmin priests at Thai court, spirit house design, and deity concepts show Hindu influence.

Indian Hindu traders and priests brought Shaiva and Vaishnava traditions to Bali from 1st century CE. Prasasti inscriptions from 9th century confirm established Hindu kingdoms. Majapahit refugees (1478) reinforced Hindu culture.

Balinese Hinduism absorbed Buddhist elements through centuries of Hindu-Buddhist coexistence in Javanese kingdoms (Majapahit, Sailendra). Buddhist Padmasana shrines found alongside Hindu temples throughout Bali.
Over a millennium of Chinese rule introduced Taoist concepts to Vietnamese folk religion. Mother Goddess hierarchy, palace cosmology, and spirit medium practices show Taoist structural influence.
Confucian ethics of filial piety reinforced existing Vietnamese ancestor worship. Confucian scholarly culture shaped the literary and ritual dimensions of Dao Mau practice.

Buddhist concepts of compassion and karma integrated with Vietnamese folk beliefs. Dao Mau temples often include Buddhist altars. Many practitioners simultaneously follow Buddhism and Mother Goddess worship.

Caodaism emerged from Vietnamese spiritual culture, incorporating spiritism from Dao Mau medium traditions. Founder Ngo Van Chieu drew on Vietnamese folk religion's spirit communication practices alongside Buddhist, Taoist, and Christian elements.
Caodaism explicitly incorporates Buddhist karma, rebirth, and vegetarian ethics. Sakyamuni Buddha is honored as a spiritual figure within the Cao Dai pantheon.

Caodaism adopts Taoist cosmology, spiritual cultivation practices, and deified figures including Li Bai (Ly Thai Bach). Taoist occult practices informed the seance methods central to Caodai revelation.
Caodaism incorporates Confucian ethical precepts, filial piety, and social hierarchy. Confucius is honored as a spiritual figure in the Cao Dai pantheon.

Caodaism borrowed Catholic hierarchical organization (Pope, Cardinals, Bishops), the All-Seeing Eye symbol, and concept of divine revelation. French colonial Catholicism was a major influence on the founders.

Hindu-Buddhist kingdoms (Mataram, Majapahit) provided the theological layer of Kejawen. Sanskrit terminology, deity concepts (Dewi Sri, Batara Guru/Shiva), and meditation practices entered Javanese mysticism through Indian influence.

Buddhist meditation techniques and concepts of enlightenment entered Javanese culture through Sailendra dynasty (8th-9th century). Borobudur temple reflects Buddhist-Javanese synthesis that influenced Kejawen.

Sufi Islam arrived via traders in 15th century and blended with Javanese mysticism through the Wali Songo (Nine Saints). Sufi emphasis on inner spiritual journey (batin) matched Javanese kebatinan perfectly.

Hindu-Buddhist cultural influence reached Philippines through Sri Vijaya and Majapahit trade networks. Bathala may derive from Sanskrit Bhattara (noble lord). Agusan gold image shows Hindu deity style. Philippine scripts derived from Indic scripts.

Ayyavazhi emerged from Hindu theological matrix, using Vaishnavite avatar concept (Vaikundar as incarnation of Narayana). The Akilam scripture references Hindu puranic cosmology extensively while reinterpreting it.

Lingayatism arose as a radical reform of Hindu Shaivism, retaining devotion to Shiva while rejecting Vedic authority, caste system, and temple worship. Basavanna's vachanas explicitly critique orthodox Hindu practices.

connections.note: Muism and the existing korean_shamanism entry represent the same tradition at different depths. Muism is the expanded scholarly treatment with deeper coverage of Dangun mythology, Sanshin, and mudang practices.

connections.evidence: Buddhism arrived in Korea in 372 CE and absorbed many shamanic deities as dharma protectors. Sanshin shrines appear in Buddhist temple compounds. Buddhist Seonangdang and shamanic practices coexist at many sacred sites.
Confucian ancestor veneration reinforced existing Korean ancestral practices in Muism. However, Joseon-era Neo-Confucian orthodoxy actively suppressed mudang shamanism as superstition.

Scythian and Zoroastrian traditions share Indo-Iranian fire worship (Tabiti/Atar), sky-god concepts, and ritual practices. Achaemenid-Scythian cultural exchange documented by Herodotus. Some scholars view Scythian religion as a parallel Iranian development.
Capitoline Triad (Jupiter-Juno-Minerva) directly derived from Etruscan Tinia-Uni-Menrva; haruspicy adopted as official Roman divination; Etruscan kings ruled Rome 616-509 BCE
Greek colonization of southern Italy caused massive Etruscan adoption of Greek mythological narratives while maintaining distinct Etruscan religious practices, especially divination
Dionysus and Orpheus attributed Thracian origins by Greeks; Bendis given official Athenian cult c.430 BCE; Sabazios widely worshipped in Hellenistic world
Orphism attributed to Thracian Orpheus; Thracian beliefs in soul immortality (Zalmoxis) paralleled Orphic metempsychosis doctrine
Shared Celtic deities (Lugus, Epona, Matres) with distinct Iberian indigenous layer (Endovelicus, Ataecina); Celtiberian language and inscriptions show Celtic linguistic stratum
Roman conquest led to interpretatio romana of Iberian deities; Ataecina equated with Proserpina; Endovelicus survived into Roman period with Latin votive inscriptions
Mycenaean takeover of Knossos c.1450 BCE; Potnia title, snake goddess imagery, bull cult elements absorbed into Mycenaean religion; Mycenaean Linear B records at Knossos
Linear B attestations of Zeus, Poseidon, Dionysus, Artemis, Hermes directly continued into Classical Greek religion; Eleusis cult site shows Mycenaean continuity
Indo-European sky god *Dyeus became Di-wo (Zeus) in Linear B; IE thunder god cognates; warrior aristocracy religion merged with pre-Greek Minoan substrate
Ukko parallels with Thor (thunder god with hammer/axe); shared vocabulary for religious concepts; Norse sagas mention Finnish sorcery (seidr connections); geographic proximity and trade contacts
Ukko parallels with Lithuanian Perkunas; shared Finno-Baltic contact zone; borrowed religious terminology; similar thunder god mythology

Papaeus as cognate of *Dyeus Ph2ter (Sky Father); IE fire cult reflected in Tabiti worship; Scythian language was Iranian branch of Indo-European; tripartite social structure reflected in divine hierarchy
Greek colonies on Black Sea coast; Herodotus' ethnographic account; syncretic Scytho-Greek art in kurgan burials; Greek equation of Scythian deities with Olympians
Eleusinian rites built on Demeter-Persephone myth from core Greek religion; Mycenaean-era origins at Eleusis site; Homeric Hymn to Demeter provides mythological foundation

Shared soteriological concerns (blessed afterlife for initiates); Persephone central to both; Orphic gold tablets reference Eleusinian-type underworld geography; mutual influence on afterlife theology
Neoplatonists adopted Orphic cosmogony; Proclus extensively commentated on Orphic theogony; soma-sema doctrine influenced Plotinus' view of embodiment; Orphic Hymns used in Neoplatonic theurgy

Mithraism functioned within Roman religious framework; Sol Invictus Mithras title; military spread through legions; planetary symbolism drawn from Roman astrology; communal meals paralleled Roman religious feasting

Name Mithras derives from Zoroastrian Mithra; Persian grade in initiation hierarchy; however Franz Cumont's direct Iranian origin theory is now disputed — Roman Mithraism may be largely independent creation using Iranian imagery
Isis, Osiris, Horus, Anubis all derive from Egyptian religion; Ptolemaic rulers deliberately Hellenized Egyptian Isis cult; Serapis created as syncretic Osiris-Apis deity for Greek-Egyptian synthesis

Isis identified with Demeter, Aphrodite, Athena; Isis aretalogies claim she encompasses all goddesses; Hellenistic mystery format adopted for Isiac initiation; spread through Mediterranean trade routes

Augustine deeply influenced by Plotinus; Pseudo-Dionysius adopted Neoplatonic hierarchies; Cappadocian Fathers used Neoplatonic language; doctrine of divine simplicity and negative theology in Christian theology derive from Neoplatonic One
Shared emanationist cosmology; Hermetic texts circulated among same late antique intellectual circles; both practiced theurgy; Iamblichus discussed Hermes Trismegistus alongside Neoplatonic theology
Cathar dualism derives ultimately from Manichaean cosmological framework via Bogomil transmission; two-god theology, rejection of material world, ascetic elite class all parallel Manichaean structure
Gnostic demiurge concept paralleled Cathar evil creator; spiritual spark trapped in matter; knowledge/gnosis as path to liberation; medieval Cathars viewed as continuation of ancient Gnostic dualism

Kabbalah developed within rabbinic Judaism as esoteric interpretation of Torah; built on earlier Merkabah and Hekhalot mysticism (2nd-6th century); sefirot as map of divine attributes within monotheistic framework

Ein Sof parallels the Neoplatonic One; emanation of sefirot mirrors Neoplatonic procession; medieval Jewish philosophers in Islamic Spain accessed Neoplatonic texts; Keter (Crown) as first emanation parallels Nous
Druze faith emerged from Fatimid Ismaili Shi'ism; al-Hakim was Fatimid caliph; seven prophets include Muhammad; however Druze separated from Islam altogether with doctrine of al-Hakim's divinity

Five cosmic principles (intelligence, soul, word, precedent, immanence) parallel Neoplatonic emanation hierarchy; Ismaili philosophy through which Druze theology developed was deeply influenced by Neoplatonism
Seven divine emanations parallel Zoroastrian Amesha Spentas; Kurdish pre-Islamic religion had Zoroastrian elements; fire veneration; dualistic tendencies; annual New Year festival parallels Nowruz

Sheikh Adi was a Sufi mystic before founding/reforming Yazidism; Islamic vocabulary and concepts present; Yazidi caste names (Sheikh, Pir) derive from Islamic terminology; however Yazidism maintains pre-Islamic core

Mandaeism is the only surviving Gnostic religion; light-darkness dualism; demiurge (Ptahil) creates flawed material world; uthras as divine emanations; soul's ascent through cosmic spheres; rejection of material world

Adam, Seth, Noah, Shem as prophets (shared Abrahamic figures); Jordan River baptism tradition; anti-Jewish polemic suggests close proximity and deliberate differentiation; possible origin among heterodox Jewish baptist sects
Egyptian artifacts found at Knossos; shared goddess iconography; trade contacts between Crete and Egypt well attested; some scholars see parallels between Minoan Great Goddess and Isis/Hathor

Isis identified with Demeter; both mysteries centered on dying/descending deity and rebirth; similar initiation structures; Isis cult adopted Greek mystery religion format as vehicle for Egyptian theology
Joseph Smith claimed angelic revelation restoring original Christianity, incorporating Christian scripture alongside new texts
Bible Student movement emerged from Protestant Christianity with distinct non-Trinitarian interpretation
Mirza Ghulam Ahmad claimed to fulfill Islamic eschatological prophecies within Islamic framework
Emerged from Japanese religious context, originally classified as Sect Shinto though claims independent divine origin
Deguchi Nao's revelations from Ushitora no Konjin emerged from Shinto spiritual tradition
Rastafari draws heavily from Ethiopian Orthodox Christianity, Old Testament symbolism, and Afrocentric biblical interpretation
Rastafari theology incorporates strong Old Testament themes including the Nazirite vow, the Ark of the Covenant, Zion, and Israelite identity
Gerald Gardner drew on Celtic seasonal calendar and mythology alongside Western esoteric traditions
Wiccan deity concepts and some ritual elements draw on Germanic pagan symbolism
Sun Myung Moon claimed to complete Christ's unfinished mission, developing a new messianic theology rooted in Christianity
Nation of Islam uses Islamic terminology and claims Islamic identity but with heterodox theology significantly divergent from mainstream Islam
Falun Gong incorporates Buddhist concepts of karma, cultivation, and the Dharma wheel alongside Taoist qigong
Falun Gong qigong exercises and Taoist energy cultivation concepts are central to practice
Soka Gakkai is rooted in Nichiren Buddhism, specifically devotion to the Lotus Sutra
Direct reconstruction of pre-Christian Norse and Germanic religious practices based on Eddas, sagas, and archaeological evidence
Neo-Druidry revives and reimagines Celtic druidic spiritual practices, though heavily filtered through 18th-century Romanticism
Direct reconstruction of ancient Egyptian religious worship, ritual, and ethical framework based on Egyptological scholarship
Systematic revival of ancient Hellenic polytheistic worship based on ancient literary and archaeological sources
Tenrikyo as an earlier Japanese new religion helped establish patterns that influenced Oomoto's development

syncreticNodes.name: Serapis Cult
syncreticNodes.description: Ptolemaic fusion of Osiris+Apis with Zeus+Hades+Asclepius+Dionysus+Helios to unite Greek rulers and Egyptian subjects

syncreticNodes.name: Gandhara Greco-Buddhist Art
syncreticNodes.description: First anthropomorphic representations of the Buddha using Greek artistic conventions; Herakles as Vajrapani

syncreticNodes.name: Interpretatio Romana/Graeca
syncreticNodes.description: Systematic identification of foreign gods with Roman/Greek equivalents across the empire

syncreticNodes.name: Roman Mithraism
syncreticNodes.description: Roman mystery cult drawing on Iranian Mithra but substantially transformed

syncreticNodes.name: Isis Mystery Cult
syncreticNodes.description: Egyptian Isis adopted throughout Greco-Roman world as universal mother goddess

syncreticNodes.name: Shinbutsu-shugo (Shinto-Buddhist Syncretism)
syncreticNodes.description: Kami identified as manifestations of Buddhist deities (honji suijaku theory)

syncreticNodes.name: Three Teachings Harmony (Sanjiao Heyi)
syncreticNodes.description: Chinese philosophical tradition viewing Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism as complementary

syncreticNodes.name: African Diaspora Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Forced syncretism of West African religions with Catholic saints during slavery: orishas mapped to saints

syncreticNodes.name: Andean Catholic Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Inca/Andean deities merged with Catholic saints; Pachamama with Virgin Mary, Inti with Christ

syncreticNodes.name: Maya-Catholic Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Maya religious practices survived colonialism blended with Catholic forms

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Axial Age (Achsenzeit)
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Period c. 800-200 BCE when parallel breakthroughs in philosophical and religious thought occurred independently in Greece, Israel, India, China, and Persia. Shift from localized concerns toward transcendence, critical self-awareness, universal morality.
scholarlyFrameworks.critiques: Debated: some scholars question the common denominator and the exclusion of other regions; others (Robert Bellah) have expanded the concept
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Temporal clustering node showing simultaneous independent religious innovations

scholarlyFrameworks.name: The Monomyth / Hero's Journey
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Universal pattern underlying hero myths across all cultures: departure, initiation, return. One underlying story structure connecting all world mythologies through a 'collective unconscious'.
scholarlyFrameworks.critiques: Criticized for oversimplification, Western bias, ignoring cultural specificity; largely rejected in contemporary academic religious studies but enormously influential in popular culture
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Cross-cultural thematic connection layer showing shared mythological patterns

scholarlyFrameworks.name: The Sacred and the Profane
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Religion emerges from experiences of hierophany (manifestation of the Sacred). Sacred space and time give structure and orientation to the world. Myths provide models for cosmic order. Builds on Rudolf Otto's concept of the numinous.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Conceptual framework for understanding what all these traditions share: responses to the sacred

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Tripartite Ideology
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Indo-European societies organized around three functions: sovereignty (priests/kings), military force (warriors), and fertility/production (farmers/herders). These three functions are reflected in the pantheon structure.
scholarlyFrameworks.examples: Vedic: Mitra-Varuna / Indra / Ashvins. Norse: Odin-Tyr / Thor / Freyr-Freyja. Roman: Jupiter / Mars / Quirinus.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Structural analysis layer for Indo-European traditions showing parallel pantheon organization

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Historical-Linguistic Approach to Mythology
scholarlyFrameworks.scholars: Max Muller (19th c.), Calvert Watkins, J.P. Mallory, Michael Witzel
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Uses linguistic cognates to reconstruct proto-mythological concepts. Cognate deity names (Zeus/Jupiter/Dyaus/Tyr/Dievas) prove common origin. Literary formulas and mythological motifs can be traced through comparative philology of Indo-European daughter languages.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Edge annotations showing linguistic evidence for connections between traditions

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Archaeological Evidence for Religious Evolution
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Material culture (temples, ritual objects, burial practices, iconography) provides evidence for religious practices independent of texts. Key sites: Gobekli Tepe (c. 9500 BCE, earliest known monumental religious site), Catalhoyuk, Ugarit, Khvalynsk (proto-IE sacrificial evidence), Indus Valley seal iconography.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Geographic anchor points and temporal markers grounded in physical evidence

theologicalTypeDefinitions.henotheism: Worship of one god without denying existence of others
theologicalTypeDefinitions.monolatry: Exclusive worship of one god while acknowledging others exist
theologicalTypeDefinitions.pantheism: God is identical with the universe/nature
theologicalTypeDefinitions.panentheism: God encompasses and transcends the universe
theologicalTypeDefinitions.monism: Ultimate reality is one unified substance
theologicalTypeDefinitions.nontheism: Neither affirms nor denies deities; focus on practice/philosophy
theologicalTypeDefinitions.animism: Belief that all things (including nature) possess spirits
theologicalTypeDefinitions.shamanism: Spiritual practice involving altered states to communicate with spirit world
theologicalTypeDefinitions.ancestor_worship: Veneration of deceased family members as spiritual entities
